AN ORDINANCE FIXING THE PRICE OF LICENSE FOR VEHICLES RUNNING WITHIN THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE. APPROVED MARCH 12, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That no vehicle shall be run within the city of Louisville for profit or hire without a license therefor being first granted.

The price of license for one year to be paid into the Sinking Fund of said city shall be as follows, to-wit:

For each and every wagon, cart, dray, omnibus, or other vehicle not specially designated herein, drawn by a single animal, five dollars; drawn by two animals, ten dollars; drawn by three animals, fifteen dollars; drawn by four animals, twenty dollars. For each and every hack, coupe, hearse, or coach, ten dollars. For each and every buggy and carriage, two dollars.

§ 2. If any vehicle shall be run within said city for profit or hire without being licensed so to run, the owner shall be fined not less than five nor more than twenty dollars.

§ 3. Ordinance No. 493, approved July 11, 1871, and all other ordinances in conflict herewith, are hereby repealed.

---

AN ORDINANCE ESTABLISHING A LICENSE ON DOGS AND BITCHES. APPROVED MARCH 12, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That hereafter it shall be unlawful for any person or persons to own, keep, or harbor on his own or their premises any dog or bitch of the canine species without first obtaining a license therefor, as follows: For each dog there shall be paid an annual license of two dollars, and for each bitch there shall be paid annually a license of two dollars.

§ 2. Upon the payment of said license the Sinking Fund Com-

GENERAL ORDINANCES. 149

AN ORDINANCE REGULATING PUBLIC PRINTING OF THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE. APPROVED APRIL 18, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That the General Council shall, in the month of May, 1885, and annually thereafter, elect two daily newspapers published in the city of Louisville, one printed in the English language, and one printed in the German language, to do the public advertising of the city of Louisville.

§ 2. The compensation to be allowed for such advertising shall not exceed seventy-five cents per square.

§ 3. Every bill for public advertising presented to the General Council shall have appended to it the affidavit of one of the publishers of the newspaper presenting the same, showing that the bill presented is true and correct and has never been paid, and that the rate charged does not exceed seventy-five cents per square.

§ 4. All ordinances and resolutions heretofore enacted on this subject be and the same are hereby repealed.

§ 5. This ordinance is to take effect from its passage.

---

AN ORDINANCE TO LICENSE BREWERS OF MALT LIQUORS.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That every person, firm, or corporation who brews beer, ale, porter, or fermented liquors of any name or description, for sale, from malt wholly or in part, or from any substitute therefor, within this city, shall be deemed a brewer, and shall pay a license into the Sinking Fund.

§ 2. The rate of license for a brewer shall not be less than fifty dollars nor more than five hundred dollars per annum.

§ 3. Any person, firm, or corporation paying a license of fifty dollars shall be authorized to brew not exceeding 4,000 barrels, or the equivalent thereof. Any person, firm, or corporation who shall brew exceeding 4,000 barrels, or the equivalent thereof, shall pay one dollar for every 100 barrels, or fraction thereof, in addition thereto: *Provided, however*, that no license shall exceed $500.

AN ORDINANCE TO REGULATE THE PRINTING AND PUBLICATION OF MATTERS REQUIRED BY THE CHARTER OR ORDINANCES TO BE PRINTED AND PUBLISHED. APPROVED APRIL 18, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: The General Council shall, in the month of May, 1885, and annually thereafter, elect two daily papers, one printed in the English language and one printed in the German language, legally eligible to do the public advertising.

§ 2. The compensation to be allowed for the city advertising shall not exceed the lowest cash rates paid by yearly advertisers for the same amount of space.

§ 3. Every bill for public advertising presented to the General Council by such papers shall have appended to it the affidavit of one of the publishers of the newspaper presenting the bill, stating that the affiant is one of the publishers of said newspaper, and that the bill presented is at the lowest cash rates paid by yearly advertisers for the same amount of space, and has never been paid.

§ 4. An ordinance entitled "An ordinance to regulate the public printing," approved May 7, 1883, and all other ordinances and resolutions in conflict herewith are hereby repealed.

GENERAL ORDINANCES. 151

AN ORDINANCE IN RELATION TO POSTING BILLS, ADVERTISEMENTS, ETC., ON POLES SUPPORTING TELEGRAPH, TELEPHONE, ELECTRIC LIGHT, OR FIRE-ALARM WIRES. APPROVED MAY 4, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That it shall be unlawful for any person or persons to nail or paste any bill, poster, placard, or advertisement to any post or pole used for supporting telegraph, telephone, electric light, or fire-alarm wires, provided said poles be kept painted to a point at least ten feet from the surface of the street; or upon any tree-box, tree-guard, or upon any tree.

§ 2. Any person or persons violating the provisions of the first section of this ordinance, on conviction thereof shall be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor, and shall be fined for each offense not less than five nor more than fifteen dollars.

---

AN ORDINANCE AUTHORIZING THE ISSUE OF DISTRAINT WARRANTS AND NOTICES OF GARNISHMENT OF RENTS FOR TAXES, AND FIXING THE COMPENSATION FOR ISSUING SAME. APPROVED MAY 4, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That, in accordance with sections 3 and 6 of article 4 of "An act to revise and amend the tax laws of the city of Louisville," the clerk of the Board of Councilmen is authorized to issue distraint warrants and notices of garnishments of rents for taxes.

§ 2. The said clerk shall receive ten cents for each warrant and ten cents for each garnishment issued and delivered to the receiver of city taxes issued under the said act.

GENERAL ORDINANCES.

AN ORDINANCE to authorize the Jeffersonville, Madison, and Indianapolis Railroad Company, Pennsylvania Company, lessee, to take up and relay the railroad tracks north of Main Street, between Thirteenth and Fifteenth streets, now owned and used by said company, and the other railroad companies crossing and using the bridge of the Louisville Bridge Company and other railway or transfer companies connected therewith, as provided in Section 1 of this Ordinance, and to construct additional tracks for the use of said companies. Approved July 9, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That the Jeffersonville, Madison, and Indianapolis Railroad Company (Pennsylvania Company, lessee) be, and is hereby, granted permission to take up and relay the tracks north of Main Street, and between Thirteenth and Fifteenth streets, and now owned and operated by said company and the other companies operating and using the bridge of the Louisville Bridge Company, and to construct, for the use of itself and said other railroad companies, three additional tracks and a portion of two entrance tracks on Fourteenth Street, two additional tracks across High Avenue, two additional tracks across Portland Avenue, and ten additional tracks across Columbia Street, so that the tracks of said companies shall conform to the plan thereof filed in the office of the city engineer, and identified as the plan referred to in this ordinance: *Provided, however*, That the Kentucky and Indiana Bridge Company shall have the right to cross the tracks and switches of the Jeffersonville, Madison, and Indianapolis Railroad Company (Pennsylvania Company, lessee), the Louisville Bridge Company, or any other railway or bridge company, on Portland Avenue up to the main track of the Louisville Bridge Company on Fourteenth Street, and shall have the right to connect with the tracks leading to the freight yard of the Louisville, New Albany, and Chicago Railway, between the north boundary of Portland Avenue and the south boundary of Duncan or Lytle streets, and to connect with the tracks leading to the passenger station and freight yard of the Ohio and Mississippi Railway, and with the Louisville Bridge Company's main track in Fourteenth Street, between the north boundary of Portland Avenue and the south boundary of Columbia Street,

Co. on Fourteenth Street. It specifies charges for transferring cars and mandates uniform charges and no discrimination against any railroad connecting within a defined territory. The ordinance also outlines requirements for track laying, street restoration, and maintenance, including specific planking for Portland Avenue and High Avenue.

# CHARTER AMENDMENTS

AND

## LOCAL ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY.

AN ACT FOR THE BENEFIT OF THE PUBLIC SCHOOLS OF THE COMMONWEALTH IN CITIES HAVING A POPULATION OF TWENTY THOUSAND AND OVER.

WHEREAS, The management of the night schools in the city of Louisville by the Board of Trustees of the Public Schools of said city has developed the fact that a large number of the applicants for admission are by laboring people of both sexes who are unable to read and write, have to be excluded under the present law because over the school age; and whereas, such people, ignorant from misfortune, poverty, or lack of opportunity, are children in knowledge, and manifest a laudable ambition in seeking knowledge to overcome their ignorance and become better citizens by studying and attending school at night after their toil of the day; and whereas, it is manifestly to the interest of the public at large that every person know how to read and write, without which knowledge they can not properly or fully discharge the duties of citizenship under a free and enlightened government; therefore,

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the board of trustees of the public schools of any city in this Commonwealth having a population of twenty thousand or over are hereby authorized and empowered to admit as pupils in the night schools under their direction and control such persons over the pupil age now prescribed by law and under the age of forty years, at their discretion, and under such rules, regu-
GENERAL ORDINANCES. 155

AN ORDINANCE FIXING THE PRICE OF LICENSE FOR VEHICLES RUNNING WITHIN THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE FOR PROFIT OR HIRE. APPROVED JULY 18, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That no vehicle shall be run within the city of Louisville for profit or hire without a license therefor being first granted, as hereinafter graded and classed, to-wit: The price of license for one year to be paid into the Sinking Fund of said city shall be as follows, to-wit:
For each and every wagon, cart, dray, omnibus, or other vehicle not specially designated herein, drawn by a single animal, five dollars; drawn by two animals, ten dollars; drawn by three animals, fifteen dollars; drawn by four animals, twenty dollars.
For each and every hack, coupe, hearse, or coach, ten dollars.
For each and every buggy and carriage, two dollars.

§ 2. If any vehicle shall be run within said city for profit or hire without being licensed so to run, the owner shall be fined not less than five nor more than twenty dollars.

§ 3. Ordinance No. 493, approved July 11, 1871, and ordinance No. —, with the same title hereof, approved March 12, 1885, and all other ordinances in conflict herewith, are hereby repealed.

---

AN ORDINANCE TO AUTHORIZE THE MAYOR TO BORROW FOR THE USE OF THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE THE SUM OF TWO HUNDRED THOUSAND DOLLARS. APPROVED OCTOBER 14, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That for the purpose of meeting the necessary expenses of the municipal government, until the taxes for the current fiscal year are collectible, the mayor of said city is hereby authorized and requested to borrow for, and in the name of the city of Louisville, a sum of money not to exceed two hundred thousand dollars in amount, for not exceeding five months, and at a rate of interest not to exceed six per centum per annum.

§ 2. For said purpose the mayor shall execute and deliver the note or notes of the city, signed by its mayor, with the seal of the city thereto affixed, and report his action in the premises to the General Council.

classification.title: AN ACT FOR THE BENEFIT OF THE PUBLIC SCHOOLS OF THE COMMONWEALTH IN CITIES HAVING A POPULATION OF TWENTY THOUSAND AND OVER.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the board of trustees of public schools in cities with a population of twenty thousand or over to admit pupils over the prescribed school age but under forty years old into night schools, at their discretion. This is to accommodate laboring individuals who are unable to read and write and were previously excluded due to age restrictions, recognizing the benefit to public interest of an educated citizenry.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct sections. The first section is an act from 1888 concerning public schools. It allows for the admission of individuals over the standard school age into night schools, up to the age of forty, if they are unable to read and write. The stated purpose is to benefit the public by increasing literacy among laboring individuals. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory intent. The second and third sections are ordinances from 1885 related to vehicle licensing and city borrowing in Louisville. These also do not contain any racial indicators or discriminatory provisions. Therefore, neither section is a Jim Crow law.

AN ACT TO AMEND ARTICLE I OF CHAPTER 47, ENTITLED "GAMING."

§ 1. That whoever, with or without compensation, shall set up, carry on or conduct, or shall aid and assist in setting up, carrying on, or conducting, a keno bank, faro bank, or other machine or contrivance used in betting, whereby money or other thing may be won or lost; or whoever shall, for compensation, percentage, or commission, set up, conduct, or carry on a game of cards, whereby money or other thing may be won or lost, or shall, with or without compensation, percentage, or commission, aid and abet in setting up, conducting, or carrying on a game so set up, carried on, or conducted for compensation, percentage, or commission—shall be fined five hundred dollars and costs, and confined in the Penitentiary not less than one nor more than three years, shall be deemed infamous after conviction, and be forever thereafter disqualified from exercising the right of suffrage, and from holding any office of honor, trust, or profit, whether it be State, county, city, or municipal. The judgment of conviction in every case shall recite such infamy and disqualification, and shall not be valid without such recital. The provisions of this section shall not include nor be applicable to persons who play at such games, unless they take other part in setting up, conducting, or carrying on such games.

§ 2. The change of the name of any of the games, tables, machines, or contrivances included in this act shall not prevent the conviction of any person violating the provisions thereof.

§ 3. An indictment for violating any of the provisions of sections 1 and 2 may charge the accused in one count with the commission of any or all of the offenses set forth in this act.

§ 4. Procedure in cases arising under this act shall be the same as prescribed by the Criminal Code of Practice in other cases of

GENERAL ORDINANCES.

AN ORDINANCE CREATING THE OFFICE OF CITY WEIGHER AND INSPECTOR, DEFINING HIS DUTIES, AND REGULATING THE FEES OF HIS OFFICE. APPROVED NOVEMBER 12, 1885.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That in the month of November the General Council of the city of Louisville shall, in joint session, elect a city weigher and inspector, whose term of office shall expire in four years from his election, or whenever his successor shall be elected and qualified; and every four years thereafter said General Council shall elect a city weigher and inspector, to serve four years, or until his successor be elected and qualified.

§ 2. Such office shall be styled the city weigher and inspector of the city of Louisville, and shall have and keep his office in the center of the business portion of the city, or of that portion wherein his services shall most likely be required, or as near thereto as practicable, and he shall be in attendance at the same time, either in person or by deputy, daily throughout the year (excepting on National or State holidays and on Christmas day), prepared to discharge all his official duties, during the months of December, January, and February, from 8 o'clock A. M. until 5 o'clock P. M., and during the other months of the year from 7:30 o'clock A. M. until 6 o'clock P. M.

§ 3. He shall provide for his use, and at his own expense, an office, suitable scales, beams, balances, and weights, tested and approved by the sealer of weights and measures for the county of Jefferson and city of Louisville, and, when requested, shall weigh any article of commerce and deliver to the applicant a certificate of the weight thereof; keep a register, in a suitable book, of all articles weighed, with the date, number of pounds, the kind of article weighed, by and for whom weighed, and the amount of fee received by him for such weighing.

§ 4. The city weigher shall cause all the weights, scales, and balances used by him in his business to be tested by the sealer of weights and measures for the county of Jefferson and city of Louisville at least once in every six months, and oftener if necessary, and receive a certificate thereof, to be preserved and filed by him, all to be done at his own expense.

§ 5. Within one week after his election, the city weigher, before

classification.title: AN ACT TO AMEND ARTICLE I OF CHAPTER 47, ENTITLED "GAMING."
classification.summary: This act amends Article I of Chapter 47 concerning gaming. It defines and penalizes the operation of keno banks, faro banks, and other betting machines or contrivances. It also addresses games of cards played for compensation, percentage, or commission. Penalties include fines, imprisonment in the penitentiary, being deemed infamous, and disqualification from suffrage and holding office. The act specifies that players are not included unless they participate in setting up or conducting the games. It also allows for charging multiple offenses in a single indictment and outlines procedural rules.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed by the legislature. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about gaming and lacks any racial component.

AN ACT TO AMEND TITLE 19, CHAPTER 4, OF THE CODE OF PRACTICE IN CIVIL CASES.

§ 1. That the receiver of the Louisville Chancery Court may, by and with the approval of said court, appoint one or more deputies, who shall take the oath required of the receiver of said court; and the said receiver may require of such deputy bond with surety for the faithful discharge of his duties. Said deputy to be removable at the pleasure of the receiver.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved April 7, 1886.

AN ACT TO AMEND AN ACT, ENTITLED "AN ACT TO ESTABLISH A STATE BOARD OF HEALTH."

§ 1. That the acts to which this is an amendment shall be so amended as to make it the duty of the council of every city in this State of ten thousand inhabitants or more to appoint a board of health for such city, to consist of six persons not members of such council, who shall be appointed as follows: Two persons for a term of one year, two persons for a term of two years, and two persons for a term of three years, and at least three of whom shall be competent physicians. The mayor of such city shall be *ex officio* a member of such board of health. Upon the expiration of the term of office of any member of a board of health appointed under this act, his successor shall be appointed for a term of three years.

The last class, condemned, shall comprise such as the city weigher and inspector shall be satisfied was baled with a view (by the party baling or causing it to be baled) of defrauding purchasers by the incorporation of foreign substances in such bale to increase the weight thereof. classification.title: AN ACT TO AMEND AN ACT, ENTITLED "AN ACT TO ESTABLISH A STATE BOARD OF HEALTH."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act establishing a State Board of Health. It mandates that city councils in cities with 10,000 or more inhabitants appoint a board of health consisting of six persons, not members of the council. Two members are appointed for one year, two for two years, and two for three years. At least three members must be competent physicians. The mayor serves as an ex officio member. Successors are appointed for three-year terms.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains several distinct legislative acts. The first two are short amendments to existing laws. The third, however, is an act to amend the establishment of a State Board of Health. This act details the composition and appointment process for city health boards in cities with populations over 10,000. It specifies the number of members, their terms, and the requirement for physician members. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory provisions within this specific act. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an excerpt from legislative acts passed during a session.

entries.ocr_text:
8 CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS.

It shall be the duty of the board of trustees or council of every incorporated city, town, or village in this State of less than ten thousand and more than two thousand five hundred inhabitants, to appoint a board of health for such city, town, or village, to consist of three persons not members of such board of trustees or council, who shall hold their office for a term of three years and until their successors have been appointed. Such boards of health shall organize within ten days after their appointment, and shall elect a competent physician, who shall be the health officer of such city, town, or village, and the executive officer of and *ex officio* a member of such board of health. At the first regular meeting of the local boards of health for the various counties of this State, after the passage of this act, it shall be the duty of such board to appoint a competent physician who shall be the health officer of such county and the executive officer of and *ex officio* a member of such board of health. This section shall not be so construed as to remove any of the existing boards of health or health officers in this State, but the successors of such boards and officers shall be appointed as in this section provided.

§ 2. A health officer appointed under this act shall hold his office for a term of two years, at the pleasure of the board making the appointment, and until his successor has been elected, and shall receive such compensation for his services as may be fixed by the board of health, and approved by the council, board of trustees, or county court of such city, town, village, or county; and in case of the death, resignation, or removal of such health officer, such board of health shall, within ten days thereafter, fill such vacancy as hereinbefore provided. Immediately upon the appointment of a health officer under this act, the board of health making the same shall transmit to the office of the State Board of Health the name and post-office address of such health officer.

§ 3. It shall be the duty of the boards of health constituted under this act to make quarterly, and such other reports as may be required by the State Board of Health, on forms to be prepared and furnished by such State Board, and all the duties, powers, and authority now conferred by law on local boards of health are hereby confirmed and made equally applicable, and in force on the city, town, or village boards of health constituted under this act, and such boards of health shall be entitled to all the assistance

§ 13. Any person changing the marks or brands of the inspector of hay, either as to weight or quality, shall, upon conviction thereof, be fined not less than $20 nor more than $100.

§ 14. Any person who shall have had any article inspected or weighed as aforesaid, and received a certificate of the inspection and weight thereof, who shall sell a part thereof and afterwards sell or offer to sell the remainder as for the quantity called for in his certificate, or shall change, alter, or in any manner falsify the certificate of the inspector and weigher, or shall suffer any of these things to be done, shall, upon conviction thereof, be fined not less than $20 nor more than $100 for each offense.

classification.title: Amendments to the Charter - 1888
classification.summary: This section of the 1888 Amendments to the Charter outlines the establishment and duties of local boards of health in incorporated cities, towns, and villages with populations between 2,500 and 10,000 inhabitants. It mandates the appointment of a three-person board of health, the election of a physician as health officer, and outlines their responsibilities in public health matters. It also details the appointment process for county health officers. The act confirms existing boards and officers and specifies terms of office, compensation, and reporting requirements to the State Board of Health. The latter part of the provided text, starting from § 12, appears to be unrelated to health boards and discusses regulations for weighers and inspectors of hay and straw, including fees and penalties for fraud.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the establishment and functions of local boards of health and health officers in Kentucky cities, towns, villages, and counties. It also includes unrelated sections on the weighing and inspection of hay and straw. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

AN ACT REGULATING THE BUSINESS OF PAWNBROKERS IN CERTAIN CITIES OF THE STATE OF KENTUCKY.

§ 1. That hereafter every person doing business as a pawnbroker in any city in this State of a population of ten thousand or over shall daily, by 11 o'clock A. M., make and furnish to the chief of police of said city, in writing, a true and correct report of all goods received by such pawnbroker during the twenty-four hours preceding each report, whether by pawn or purchase, describing the goods as accurately as practicable.

§ 2. The chief of police shall furnish blanks for reports herein provided for.

§ 3. For a failure upon the part of any pawnbroker to comply with the provisions of this act, he shall be fined not less than twenty dollars nor more than one hundred dollars for each offense, in the discretion of a jury.

§ 4. This act shall take effect and be in force from its passage.

Approved April 22, 1886.

---

§ 1. That any person in this State finding and bringing ashore the dead body of any human being from any navigable stream coursing along and through this State, shall receive a fee or reward not exceeding five dollars. The said fee or reward to be paid by the county in which such dead or drowned person is found: *Provided, however*, The coroner of such county shall truly certify to such claim after an inquest is had, according to law, by said coroner.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

AN ACT TO AMEND CHAPTER 84 OF THE GENERAL STATUTES, TITLE "PEDDLERS."

§ 1. That the provisions of chapter 84 of the General Statutes, title "Peddlers," shall not be construed to extend to farmers vending, or offering to vend, or exposing to sale, in person or by agent, anywhere within this Commonwealth, or in any of the cities or towns thereof, goods, wares, or merchandise, or other property which is the produce of their farms.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved May 15, 1886.

---

AN ACT PROVIDING FOR FILLING VACANCIES THAT HAVE OR MAY HEREAFTER OCCUR IN THE OFFICE OF JUDGE OF THE LOUISVILLE LAW AND EQUITY COURT.

§ 1. That vacancies in the office of judge of the Louisville Law and Equity Court shall be filled at the same time, and for the same period of vacancies, and in like manner, and on like occasion, as vacancies in the office of judge of the Jefferson Circuit Court.

§ 2. But this act shall not apply to, or in any manner affect the term of present incumbent of said office of judge of the Louisville Law and Equity Court.

§ 3. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved May 15, 1886.

12 CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS.

AN ACT TO PROVIDE AGAINST DANGERS FROM CARBON AND PETROLEUM OILS, AND TO PROVIDE FOR GAUGING OF OILS, NAPHTHA, AND TURPENTINE.

§ 1. That all oils and fluids, the product of coal, petroleum, or other bituminous substances, by whatever name called, which may or can be used for illuminating purposes, manufactured in this State or brought into it before the same is consumed, used, or sold to merchants or consumers within this State, shall be inspected by an authorized inspector of this State.

§ 2. The judges of the county court of each county of this State may appoint an inspector in their respective counties to carry out the provisions of this law. The inspector shall remain in office for four years, unless removed by the court for misconduct, negligence, or incompetency, and shall take an oath faithfully and to the best of his ability to discharge the duties of his office. An inspector may appoint an assistant or deputy, who may perform, under his direction, and for such times as he may designate, any and all the duties of an inspector under this act.

§ 3. An inspector, in the performance of his duties, shall use the standard instruments in use for that purpose, and shall test all oils as follows:

First. The water cup shall have sufficient water in it to rise two-thirds up the side of the oil cup.

Second. Fill the oil cup with oil to be tested to within one-eighth of an inch of the top.

Third. Suspend the thermometer so the bulb is just under the surface of the oil.

Fourth. Use an alcohol lamp to heat the water-bath, and before placing the light under the water cup, test the oil in the oil cup by bringing a lighted match in contact with the surface of the oil. If it does not ignite, place the lamp under the water-cup, and slowly heat the oil, not slower than one degree of the thermometer in a minute, nor faster than two degrees of the thermometer in a minute, moving a lighted match across the surface of the oil at each degree the thermometer rises, not more than three-eighths of an inch from the surface of the oil. If the oil should flash, that is, a little gas burn on the surface and go out again, remove the lamp, and as soon as the thermometer ceases to rise, test the oil; and should it

classification.title: AN ACT TO PROVIDE AGAINST DANGERS FROM CARBON AND PETROLEUM OILS, AND TO PROVIDE FOR GAUGING OF OILS, NAPHTHA, AND TURPENTINE.
classification.summary: This act mandates the inspection of illuminating oils derived from coal, petroleum, or other bituminous substances. It empowers county court judges to appoint inspectors who will test these oils using standard instruments and procedures to ensure safety. Inspectors are appointed for four-year terms and can be removed for misconduct. They may also appoint deputies. The testing process involves specific steps for filling oil cups, suspending thermometers, and heating the oil to determine its flash point.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the inspection and safety testing of flammable oils. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The act focuses solely on public safety and regulatory measures for a specific commodity. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 13

not ignite, re-place the lamp, and test the oil each degree the thermometer rises till the oil ignites or permanently burns. As soon as the oil ignites or permanently burns, the degree indicated by the thermometer is the *fire test* of the oil. The flame moved across the surface of the oil should not exceed that of an ordinary match.

§ 4. All oils and fluids specified in section 1 of this act that ignite or permanently burn at a temperature of 130 degrees Fahrenheit and upwards, shall be approved by the inspector; and the barrels, casks, or packages containing the same shall be branded or marked by him with his name, official character, and the words, "approved fire test," and all oils or fluids aforesaid that ignite or permanently burn at a less temperature than 130 degrees Fahrenheit shall be condemned by the inspector, and the barrels, casks, or packages containing the same shall be branded or marked by him with his name, official character, and the words, "unsafe for illuminating purposes."

§ 5. If any person or persons in this State shall use or sell to merchants or consumers, within this State, any of the oils or fluids specified in section 1 of this act, without first having the same inspected by an authorized inspector of this State, and the barrels, casks, or packages containing the same branded or marked by him as provided in section 4 of this act, said person or persons so offending shall be punished by a fine of twenty dollars for each barrel, cask, or package of oils or fluids aforesaid so used or sold to merchants or consumers within this State, without same being inspected and branded. Fifty per cent. of the penalty recovered shall go to the inspector in lieu of his fees, and the balance to the State.

§ 6. If any person or persons in this State shall sell, or offer to sell, to be consumed or used in this State for illuminating purposes, any of the oils or fluids specified in section on [1] of this act, that will ignite or permanently burn at a temperature less than 130 degrees Fahrenheit, or shall sell, or offer for sale, to be consumed in this State for illuminating purposes, any of the oils and fluids aforesaid, which have been condemned by an authorized inspector of this State, and the barrels, casks, or packages containing the same be branded or marked by him "unsafe for illuminating purposes," the person or persons so offending shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, and, on conviction, be punished by a fine not exceeding one hundred dollars, and the oils and fluids be forfeited and sold, and the proceeds to go to the State.

classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Inspection of Oils and Fluids for Illuminating Purposes
classification.summary: This act establishes standards for the inspection of illuminating oils and fluids in Kentucky. It defines the 'fire test' and mandates that oils igniting at or above 130 degrees Fahrenheit are approved, while those igniting below are condemned. The inspector is required to brand approved and condemned oils. Penalties are outlined for selling uninspected or condemned oils, with fines and forfeiture of the product.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details regulations for the inspection of illuminating oils and fluids. It sets safety standards based on the flash point of these substances and outlines penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, and the regulations apply universally to all individuals and businesses involved in the sale or use of these oils within the state. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

14 CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS.

§ 7. Any dealer in, or manufacturer of, oils or fluids specified in section 1 of this act, who shall refuse to admit an inspector or his deputy upon his premises, so far as it may be necessary for the performance of his duties, or shall obstruct an inspector or his deputy in the performance of his duties, shall for each refusal to admit on his premises, or obstruction offered to inspection, be fined for each offense not less than twenty dollars nor exceeding fifty dollars.

§ 8. As full compensation for his services the inspector shall receive for inspecting said oils, in quantities in bulk of four thousand (4,000) gallons or more, ten (10) cents per barrel of fifty gallons. If inspected in barrels, the inspector shall receive twenty cents per barrel for inspecting in lots of fifty barrels or more, and thirty cents per barrel for inspecting in lots of less than fifty barrels at one time.

§ 9. All inspectors shall execute a bond to the Commonwealth of Kentucky in the sum of one thousand dollars ($1,000), with security as shall be approved by the judge of the county making the appointment, which bond shall be for the use of all persons in any way aggrieved or injured by the acts or neglect of such inspector, or any deputy or assistant appointed by an inspector under this act. The bond shall be filed in the office of the county clerk in the county of his appointment, and any one aggrieved by the acts of the inspector or his deputy or assistant may bring suit on his bond in the circuit court of the county of his appointment.

§ 10. All oils or fluids aforesaid, sent from other States into this State for consumption within the limits of this State, in the counties of this State where there are no inspectors acting under this law, or sent from counties in this State in barrels or packages not bearing the inspector's brand, as required by this law, of the county from which they were sent, may be inspected in transit in the hands of a carrier, forwarding agent, or warehouseman, or wherever found in any store or at any railway station, and the inspector may charge thirty cents per barrel for each barrel thus inspected; and it shall be the duty of the party having the oils or fluids in hand at the time of the inspection to pay the inspector his fees, and he shall have a lien on said oils or fluids until they are paid. § 11. Any inspector of any county in this Commonwealth may go into any other county in the Commonwealth where there is no acting inspector under this law, and inspect and brand, according to law, any oil or fluids aforesaid which he may find intended for consumption in this Commonwealth, which have not been inspected and branded by some authorized inspector within the State of Kentucky. The inspector may charge for his services thirty cents per barrel for each barrel of oils or fluids inspected and branded by him, and his traveling expenses going and coming by the usual way of traveling, which expenses shall not exceed five cents per mile for the distance going and coming to the place of inspection, to be paid by the party in possession of the oil or fluid at the time of the inspection, or by the owner of the oils or fluids; and the inspector shall have a lien on the oils or fluids inspected by him for his fees and traveling expenses.

§ 12. The provisions of this law shall not apply to oils or fluids brought into this Commonwealth in transit for shipment to and consumption in other States.

§ 13. The inspector of oils and fluids shall, *ex officio*, be gauger of petroleum oils, lubricating oils, naphtha, and turpentine, and in the performance of his duties shall use the standard instruments in use for that purpose; and he shall brand or mark all barrels or casks gauged by him with his name, official character, and the capacity of the package; and the inspector shall be notified of the arrival of such oils in his county within twenty-four hours after its arrival.

§ 4 [14.] All laws and parts of laws in conflict with the provisions of this act be, and the same are hereby, repealed.

AN ORDINANCE TO PROVIDE FOR THE ESTABLISHMENT AND MAINTENANCE OF A FIRE DEPARTMENT. PASSED GENERAL COUNCIL MARCH 18, 1886.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That the duties herein assigned shall be fully complied with by the chief engineer.

§ 2. He shall be present at all the fires when practicable, and see that a proper application is made of the means at his control for the extinguishment thereof.

§ 3. He is hereby clothed with full police authority in all matters relating to the department, and should an occasion occur that those belonging to the department are not sufficient to check the progress of the fire, protect surrounding property, and maintain order, he may, for the time being, employ an additional force and report the names of all persons thus employed to the General Council, to whom compensation may be allowed for such services.

§ 4. He shall require all persons connected with the department to reside within a convenient distance of the engine-house of their respective companies (which may hereafter be connected with the department.)

the same is hereby amended as follows, to-wit: That the Citizens' Gas-light Company of Louisville, Kentucky, be, and the same is hereby, authorized and empowered to make water-gas within the limits of the city of Louisville, and to vend the same for illuminating, heating, and other purposes through pipes laid in the streets to said city and its citizens: *Provided*, That this act shall not change any rights or authority the said Citizens' Gas-light Company of Louisville, Kentucky, may now have to use the streets, alleys, or public ways of the city of Louisville in laying down pipes or mains to distribute the gas so made to the purchasers of the same, or diminish any right or authority the city of Louisville now has to control or regulate such use by said Citizens' Gas-light Company through the streets, alleys, and public ways of said city.

§ 2. *And provided further*, That the Citizens' Gas-light Company is hereby prohibited from selling out to *in solido* or buying out *in solido* any rival gas company that is now or may hereafter be established within the city of Louisville.

§ 3. That said Citizens' Gas-light Company shall not charge in excess of one dollar and fifty cents ($1.50) per thousand cubic feet for gas furnished by said company to the city and the citizens of Louisville.

§ 4. This act shall be in force from and after its passage.
Approved February 13, 1886.

AN ACT CONCERNING THE COUNTY REVENUE OF JEFFERSON COUNTY.

§ 1. That the office of treasurer be, and the same is hereby, created for Jefferson County.

§ 2. That a treasurer to fill said office shall be elected by the levy court of said county at its next regular meeting in March, and every four years thereafter, for a term of four years, and until his successor is elected or appointed and qualified, commencing on the first day of April of the year in which elected: *Provided, however*, That only justices of said county from precincts outside the corporate limits of the city of Louisville shall vote at said election: *And provided also*, That a majority of said justices elect shall be present at said election.

§ 3. In case of a vacancy in said office, the county judge of

§ 5. It shall be the duty of the chief engineer to make a full and complete report to the first regular meeting of the General Council in each month of the persons on duty in the month ending with the day of report, in their respective capacities, the number of days each performed duty, the amount of pay due to each, and such other expenses as may have been incurred during the month. Said report shall be made under oath. Upon the approval of the General Council of a report of the chief engineer, and allowance of pay and expenses upon the report by resolution duly approved, it shall be the duty of the auditor to draw his warrant upon the treasurer in favor of the parties named in said pay-roll for the full amount of pay and expenses thus allowed, in accordance with an ordinance entitled "An ordinance prescribing the manner in which claims against the city of Louisville shall be made."

§ 6. He is hereby authorized, and it is made his duty to visit any house, yard, or premises in the city wherein it is known or supposed that any character of fixtures exist which may be dangerous in causing or promoting fires, and on examination thereof, should any such danger appear, he shall direct in writing the owner, agent, or occupant of the premises containing such fixtures to remove, alter, or amend the same in such manner and time as he may deem reasonable and just.

§ 7. He shall not permit racing to or from fires, and if more than one company proceed on the same street to a fire, they shall go in single file. No company shall leave the city to attend a fire in a neighboring town without the consent of the chief, nor on an excursion unless by permission of the General Council.

§ 8. He shall obey the order of the mayor and such regulations as may from time to time be adopted by the General Council.

§ 9. He shall cause the bells to be rung at 7 A. M., 12 M., 1 P. M., 6 P. M., and 10 P. M.

§ 10. The fire department herein established shall consist of as many companies (whether of steam fire-engine, chemical engine, or hook and ladder companies) as the General Council may from time to time designate by ordinance or resolution.

classification.summary: This act establishes the office of treasurer for Jefferson County, to be elected by the levy court. It specifies the election process, term of office, and procedures for filling vacancies. A key provision restricts voting in the treasurer election to justices from precincts outside the corporate limits of the city of Louisville.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative acts. The first, concerning the Citizens' Gas-light Company of Louisville, is a corporate charter amendment and does not appear to have any racial implications. The second act, 'AN ACT CONCERNING THE COUNTY REVENUE OF JEFFERSON COUNTY,' establishes a county treasurer position. While it includes a specific provision that only justices from precincts *outside* the corporate limits of the city of Louisville shall vote in the election for this treasurer, this restriction is based on geographic location (within or outside Louisville's corporate limits) and not explicitly on race. There is no mention of race or any mechanism that would inherently discriminate based on race. Therefore, neither act is classified as a Jim Crow law.

said county shall fill the same by appointment; and the person so appointed shall take and hold the same upon the same terms, and subject to the same duties, responsibilities, and liabilities, as if elected by the levy court as aforesaid, and shall continue in office until his successor is elected and qualified; and it shall be the duty of said levy court, at its next ensuing March term, to elect a person to fill the office for the unexpired term. Any one elected or appointed treasurer as aforesaid shall, within ten days thereafter, signify his acceptance thereof by a communication in writing to the county court, and give the bond herein required, which acceptance and bond shall be entered of record in said county court; and upon failure to accept said office as aforesaid, and give said bond, and have the same recorded within ten days after election or appointment, the judge of said county court shall at once declare said office vacant, and fill the same by appointment as hereinabove provided.

§ 4. It shall be the duty of said treasurer to receive, safely keep, and pay out, as hereinafter provided, all the revenue of said county.

§ 5. It shall be the duty of the sheriff, and all other officers collecting money for said county, to pay the same over to said treasurer on the following dates of each year, viz.: the first day of March, May, August, and November of each year; but any of said collecting officers may, at his option, pay over to said treasurer any money he may collect at any time prior to any of said dates. Said sheriff or other collecting officer shall, on no account, pay any part of said county revenue to any other person than said treasurer.

§ 6. Said treasurer shall pay out no money except upon a duly attested warrant, issued upon order of said levy court or upon order of such board as may be created to pass upon claims, which, by contract, are paid jointly by said city of Louisville and said county: *Provided, however*, That this shall not apply to money received by him for the support of the poor-house of said county, and that he shall pay to the person or persons authorized to receive the same, such proportion of all moneys received by him as the tax levied for poor-house purposes bears to the whole county levy.

For neglect of duty on the part of the chief engineer, charges may be preferred against him by the Board of Aldermen, in accordance with the provisions of section 8 of the city charter, and if the charges are maintained the said Board of Aldermen shall declare the office vacant. In the event of vacancy in the office of chief engineer from any cause prior to the expiration of the term for which he had been elected, the General Council shall fill the vacancy.

§ 14. The hook and ladder companies shall consist of one captain, one driver, one steersman, one fuel-cart driver, and four laddermen, who shall be on permanent duty at the hook and ladder houses to perform such duties as the chief engineer or his assistants may direct, and they shall receive the amount paid pipemen in monthly installments to each, according to the pay-roll, when approved by the General Council. The chief engineer of the fire department is authorized to appoint two assistants, to be known and designated as first and second assistants, who, if in their power, shall attend all fires happening in any part of the city, and in case of the absence or disability of the chief, it shall be the duty of the assistants to take charge of the organization, and they shall have and exercise all the powers of the chief, and in case of fire in the absence of the chief, they shall exercise his power. § 9. The clerk of the county court of said county shall, on the order book, number all orders allowing claims serially in the order in which allowed, beginning with the number one at each term of the levy court, and shall number the warrants issued thereon to correspond therewith.

§ 10. The books herein provided for shall be public records, and shall be kept in the county clerk's office; and said treasurer shall be and remain at said clerk's office on Wednesdays and Saturdays of each week, and as much oftener as necessary, for the purpose of performing the duties herein required.

§ 11. Said treasurer shall prepare and present to the levy court, on the first day of each of its terms, a report showing the aggregate of money received and paid out by him since the last term of said court, the amount of money on hand, the aggregate of unpaid warrants or orders, and the amount remaining to be paid to the poor-house, if the entire assessed tax should be collected; and with said report he shall return his vouchers for all disbursements made by him, and said report and vouchers shall be filed and remain in the clerk's office of said court. Thereupon the presiding officer of said levy court shall appoint two justices thereof from precincts outside the corporate limits of the city of Louisville, who, with the county attorney, shall constitute a committee, whose duty it shall be to examine said report, vouchers, and books of said treasurer, and report, within ten days thereafter, to the county court, whether they find the same correct or not; and if not, in what particulars they find the same incorrect; and said report shall be filed and remain in said clerk's office. Thereupon said county court shall enter an order referring to said report, and allowing or disallowing the accounts of said treasurer in accordance therewith.

§ 12. Said treasurer shall, at the expiration of his term of office, or upon resignation or removal therefrom, turn over to his successor in office all books, vouchers, and money belonging to said county then in his hands and take his receipt therefor. And upon presentation thereof to the county court, after written notice to the county attorney, shall be entitled to, and shall receive from said court, a final discharge from all liability as treasurer aforesaid, to the extent that his accounts shall have been allowed under the provisions of section 11 hereof.

If said treasurer, after ascertaining the amount of appropriations of said levy court, at either of its terms, has not enough money on hand over and above the money herein appropriated to other purposes, namely, for the poor-house, and to pay off the present indebtedness of the county, to pay off such appropriations he shall, with the approval of the county, borrow on the credit of the county such sum as, together with the money on hand, will be sufficient for said purpose; *Provided*,

1. That the aggregate of money so borrowed shall in no case exceed five thousand dollars, nor shall he receive any loan for a longer time than four months, nor at a greater interest than six per cent.
2. That the aggregate of the sums so borrowed for any year and remaining unpaid shall, in no case, exceed eighty per cent. of the difference between the county revenue for that year which has come into the treasurer's hands, and the whole amount of the county tax levied for such year, less the amount of said difference, which must, when collected, be applied to poor-house purposes and to the present indebtedness of said county.
3. That said treasurer shall apply to the payment of money so borrowed the first money thereafter coming into his hands and not herein directed to be applied for said other purposes.
4. That no money shall be borrowed as aforesaid, except within ten days from the day on which said levy court shall have adjourned.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*:
That in accordance with the provisions of an act of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, entitled "An act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville," approved March 20, 1886, the mayor is hereby authorized and directed to cause to be prepared five hundred thousand dollars of coupon bonds of said city, of the denomination of one thousand dollars each, to be dated the first day of August, 1886, and bear interest at the rate of five per centum per annum, payable semi-annually, and the principal thereof payable twenty-five years after their date; interest and principal to be paid at the National Bank of the Republic, in the city of New York. And in order to provide for the payment of the principal and interest of said bonds as they respectively fall due, a tax of five cents on each one hundred dollars' worth of property, real and personal, and investments that are liable to taxation for the Louisville Public Schools, is hereby levied and to be levied annually according to the provisions of the act aforesaid, until said bonds, principal and interest, are fully paid. The said bonds shall show on their face that they are a charge on the Sinking Fund, shall be numbered, signed by the mayor, under the corporate seal of the city, and countersigned by the auditor and registered by the secretary and treasurer of the Sinking Fund, and the coupons to be signed by the auditor alone.

So soon as the said bonds are prepared as hereinbefore directed, the mayor shall deliver the same to the commissioners of the Sinking Fund, who shall hold the same as the property of the Sinking Fund, and have the right of a *bona fide* holder, and may from time to time, as they deem advisable, sell and dispose of the same, and pay the proceeds of the sale of said bonds into the city treasury.

AN ORDINANCE TO REGULATE THE RETAILING OF FRESH MEATS IN THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE. APPROVED APRIL 13, 1886.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That hereafter it shall be unlawful for any person to sell, at retail, beef, mutton, veal, or lamb, in the city of Louisville, except in a regular licensed market-house or in a green grocery.

§ 2. Any one violating this ordinance shall be punished by a fine of not less than five nor more than twenty dollars for each offense.

§ 21. Said treasurer shall deposit all money received by him and not immediately disbursed, in such bank as said levy court may direct.

§ 22. The salary of said treasurer shall be fixed by said levy court at the time of his election: *Provided*, That it shall not exceed three hundred dollars a year: *And provided further*, That the salary so fixed shall not be reduced until another election shall be had in accordance with the provisions of this act.

§ 23. Said levy court is hereby authorized and empowered to levy each year an additional sum of five cents on each one hundred dollars' worth of taxable property in said county: *Provided*, That the power granted in this section shall cease when the present indebtedness of said county referred to in sections 17 and 18 hereof shall have been discharged, and in any event after the levy for the year 1889: *Provided*, That the power granted in this section shall not vest until the election of a treasurer as hereinbefore provided.

§ 24. Said levy court shall make the county levy at such of its regular terms as will enable the sheriff to collect the county taxes at the same time that he collects the State taxes.

§ 25. Said levy court shall, in no year, make appropriations aggregating more than eighty per cent. of the assessed taxes for such year which, under the provisions of this act, may be available for the payment of such appropriations.

§ 26. All acts or parts of acts in conflict herewith are hereby repealed in so far as they apply to Jefferson County.

§ 27. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 25, 1886.

He shall, under such control and supervision of the city attorney as the law implies, perform the work imposed on that officer by the act of May 12, 1884, entitled "An act to revise and amend the tax laws of the city of Louisville," and under the laws amendatory thereof, except as far as said act and the laws amendatory thereof relate to license taxes.

§ 3. He shall receive a salary of two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500), payable in monthly installments, and shall, before entering upon his duties under this ordinance, execute bond, to be approved by the General Council.

AN ACT TO AMEND THE CHARTER OF THE CENTRAL PASSENGER RAILROAD COMPANY.

provided for herein, and any vacancy shall be filled as now required by law or ordinance; and if a majority of the votes cast shall be for the railroad proposition, then it shall be the duty of the mayor to indorse said bonds as provided for in said petition, and the terms set forth in said petition shall be the agreement between the said city and the said railroad company; or the General Council of the city of Louisville may, by ordinance, provide for such indorsement as aforesaid upon the terms set forth therein, and duly passed as provided for in relation to other ordinances of said city. Section 2 specifically states that the city shall not subscribe to the capital stock or bonds, or indorse the bonds of certain railroad companies. It also includes provisions for voting on taxes in specific counties and districts, excluding the fifth voting district of Anderson County from tax assessment on personal property. The latter part of the document ratifies and confirms the rights of The Ohio Valley Telephone Company to operate within the city, with specific conditions regarding the use of its infrastructure for the city's fire and police systems and indemnification for damages.

CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS.

AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE ECONOMIC HEATING COMPANY.

§ 1. That Geo. W. Swearengin, John B. Castleman, N. Bloom, John G. Simrall, John A. Tanner, David W. Fairleigh, Temple Bodley, Arthur M. Rutledge, and Clarence U. McElroy, their successors and assigns, be, and they are hereby, created a body corporate, under the name of the Economic Heating Company; and by that name shall have perpetual succession, and the power to contract and be contracted with, to sue and be sued, implead and be impleaded; to take, hold, own, and in anywise alien property, real, personal, and mixed; to have a common seal, and alter the same at pleasure; and to establish by-laws, and make rules and regulations for the management of the affairs of said company, not inconsistent with the Constitution or laws of this State or of the United States.

§ 2. The affairs of said corporation shall be conducted by a board of directors of not less than five nor more than nine directors, who shall be elected for a term of one year, and until their successors are chosen, at a meeting of the stockholders of said corporation, held in the city of Louisville on the second Monday in January of each year. Said directors shall elect a president and vice-president who shall be, and a secretary and treasurer who may be, members of said board. The meetings of said board shall be called by the president, or in case of his absence or inability to act, by the vice-president, or in case of his absence also or inability to act, by a written call of not less than one-half the directors. The powers, duties, and compensation of the president and other officers of the company shall be prescribed by the board of directors. The directors and officers of the company, to serve until the second Monday in January, 1887, shall be elected by the incorporators hereinbefore named. In all meetings of the stockholders they shall vote in proportion to their stock, in person or by proxy duly authorized in writing signed by them. Of all meetings of stockholders, “whether called by the board of directors, as they may be, or the annual meetings aforesaid,” five days’ previous notice shall be given to the stockholders by insertions in a daily newspaper published in the city of Louisville.

# CONSTITUTION OF THE UNITED STATES.

We, the people of the United States, in order to form a more perfect Union, establish justice, insure domestic tranquility, provide for the common defense, promote the general welfare, and secure the blessings of liberty to ourselves and our posterity, do ordain and establish this Constitution for the United States of America.

## ARTICLE I.

### SECTION 1.

1. All legislative powers herein granted shall be vested in a Congress of the United States, which shall consist of a Senate and House of Representatives.

### SECTION 2.

1. The House of Representatives shall be composed of members chosen every second year by the people of the several States; and the electors in each State shall have the qualifications requisite for electors of the most numerous branch of the State Legislature.
2. No person shall be a Representative who shall not have attained to the age of twenty-five years, and been seven years a citizen of the United States, and who shall not, when elected, be an inhabitant of that State in which he shall be chosen.
3. Representatives and direct taxes shall be apportioned among the several States which may be included within this Union, according to their respective numbers, which shall be determined by adding to the whole number of free persons, including those bound to service for a term of years, and excluding Indians not taxed, three-fifths of all other persons. The actual enumeration shall be made within three years after the first meeting of the Congress of the United States, and within every subsequent term of ten years,

AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE CATHOLIC KNIGHTS OF AMERICA INSTITUTE COMPANY OF KENTUCKY, AT LOUISVILLE, KENTUCKY.

§ 1. That Thomas Feely, George Wolf, Newton G. Rogers, Chas. Kremer, S. B. McGill, Edward Neihaus, Henry Felhoelter, Col. G. D. Deuser, Frank Speiker, John J. Schulten, Thomas Small, W. B. Doherty, M. D., M. J. Ford, John J. Score, William Potter, John Uhrig, L. H. Bell, M. Regan, John Cosgrove, Peter J. Dowling, P. Halloran, A. Stickler, Gran. W. Smith, be, and they are hereby, their associates and successors, created a body-politic under the name and style of the Catholic Knights of America Institute Company of Kentucky, whose principal place of transacting business shall be Louisville, Kentucky, with power in the said corporation to sue and be sued, contract and be contracted with, and to acquire by purchase or otherwise real or personal estate in the city of Louisville, State of Kentucky, and to hold, improve, sell, lease, and rent the same for the purpose of affording suitable offices of any of the supreme officers of the Catholic Knights of America, and the offices of the Kentucky State Council of said order, vaults for the records of said order, and suitable halls, meeting rooms, library, reading rooms, etc., for the members of the said order, or of the said company, or other Catholic societies, for charitable and benevolent uses and purposes.

§ 2. The amount of the capital stock of the said corporation shall not exceed ($100,000) one hundred thousand dollars, and shall be divided into shares of ($5) five dollars each, and subscriptions shall be made thereto in the manner and upon the conditions that the board of directors may direct, and shall be paid in such manner and at such times as the board of directors may determine.

§ 3. The business and management of this corporation shall be conducted by a president, two vice-presidents, a board of fifteen directors, a secretary, a treasurer, and such other officers, agents,
182 CONSTITUTION OF THE UNITED STATES.

in such manner as they shall by law direct. The number of Representatives shall not exceed one for every thirty thousand; but each State shall have at least one Representative; and until such enumeration shall be made, the State of New Hampshire shall be entitled to choose three; Massachusetts, eight; Rhode Island and Providence Plantations, one; Connecticut, five; New York, six; New Jersey, four; Pennsylvania, eight; Delaware, one; Maryland, six; Virginia, ten; North Carolina, five; South Carolina, five; and Georgia, three.

4. When vacancies happen in the representation from any State, the Executive authority thereof shall issue writs of election to fill up such vacancies.

5. The House of Representatives shall choose their speaker and other officers, and shall have the sole power of impeachment.

SECTION 3.

1. The Senate of the United States shall be composed of two Senators from each State, chosen by the Legislature thereof, for six years; and each Senator shall have one vote.

2. Immediately after they shall be assembled in consequence of the first election, they shall be divided, as equally as may be, into three classes. The seats of the Senators of the first class shall be vacated at the expiration of the second year, of the second class at the expiration of the fourth year, and of the third class at the expiration of the sixth year, so that one-third may be chosen every second year; and if vacancies happen, by resignation or otherwise, during the recess of the Legislature of any State, the Executive thereof may make temporary appointments until the next meeting of the Legislature, which shall then fill such vacancies.

3. No person shall be a Senator who shall not have attained to the age of thirty years, and been nine years a citizen of the United States, and who shall not, when elected, be an inhabitant of that State for which he shall be chosen.

4. The Vice President of the United States shall be President of the Senate, but shall have no vote, unless they be equally divided.

5. The Senate shall choose their other officers, and also a President *pro tempore*, in the absence of the Vice President, or when he shall exercise the office of President of the United States.

and employes as the board of directors may from time to time determine. The board of directors may pass by-laws for its government, and prescribe the duties of its officers, agents, or employes, and may prescribe the manner in which said by-laws may be amended or repealed, and said board of directors shall be elected as follows: At the first election held for directors under this charter, the five candidates for such office receiving the lowest number of votes shall serve as such for one year; the five receiving the next highest number of votes shall serve for two years, and the five who may receive the highest number of votes, each, respectively, shall serve for three years; but all directors shall serve until their successors may be elected and accept such office; and after such election there shall annually thereafter be held an election at which five directors shall be elected to serve for three years, after notice of the time and place at which such election is to take place shall have been given by an advertisement, to be inserted once in a daily newspaper published in Louisville, Kentucky, and a similar notice shall be necessary to call together any other meeting of the stockholders; and said board of directors shall have power to suspend or expel any of the members of its board for cause sufficient to it, and to fill any vacancy or vacancies occurring in the board.

Supply Company; and are hereby authorized and empowered to supply to the households in the various towns and cities in said Commonwealth natural or artificial gas for cooking, heating, or other household purposes and domestic uses, and for manufacturing; to sue and be sued, to contract and be contracted with; to buy, lease, and hold such property, real and personal, as may be necessary to enable them to supply said gas for the purposes and uses aforementioned, and to dispose of the same at pleasure; to have and use a seal and the same to alter at will; and to have, use, and possess, and the same are hereby granted, all the rights, powers, and privileges necessary, proper, or proper to carry into effect and exercise all the powers, franchises, and privileges hereby granted.

§ 2. The capital stock of said company shall consist of fifty thousand dollars ($50,000), to be divided into shares of one hundred dollars ($100) each, and the same may be increased at any time by a majority of the present incorporators, if in their judgment the business shall demand it, or in the future by the board of directors, a majority thereof voting therefor and approved by the stockholders holding the majority of said stock, and at a meeting called for that special purpose, and each share of stock being entitled to one vote. Said stock may be paid out as non-assessable paid up stock for services rendered to or for property bought by or for work done to or for said company.

§ 3. The first meeting for the organization under the charter, and for the transaction of such business as may be done thereby, may be at such time or times as the majority of the incorporators may direct. The directors shall have the right to make the necessary by-laws for the government of said corporation in all its departments, and for the management of its property and concerns, and to fix the duties and responsibilities of its officers and employes, and to settle how and in what manner its stock may be transferred or assigned by one holder to another so as to ascertain in whose right said stock is held and owned, and to direct when and how the capital stock shall be paid in by the subscribers therefor, and to fix how and when said stock so subscribed and not paid in shall be forfeited in whole or in part, or to sue for the balance due thereon, or to provide for the sale thereof to pay the balance due thereon; and to do any and all things or acts needful and

Digitized by Google
184 CONSTITUTION OF THE UNITED STATES.

SECTION 6.

1. The Senators and Representatives shall receive a compensation for their services, to be ascertained by law, and paid out of the Treasury of the United States. They shall, in all cases, except treason, felony, and breach of the peace, be privileged from arrest during their attendance at the session of their respective Houses, and in going to or returning from the same; and for any speech or debate in either House, they shall not be questioned in any other place.

2. No Senator or Representative shall, during the time for which he was elected, be appointed to any civil office under the authority of the United States which shall have been created, or the emoluments whereof shall have been increased, during such time; and no person holding any office under the United States shall be a member of either House during his continuance in office.

SECTION 7.

1. All bills for raising revenue shall originate in the House of Representatives; but the Senate may propose or concur with amendments, as on other bills.

2. Every bill which shall have passed the House of Representatives and the Senate shall, before it become a law, be presented to the President of the United States; if he approve, he shall sign it; but if not, he shall return it, with his objections, to that House in which it shall have originated, who shall enter the objection at large on their journal, and proceed to reconsider it. If, after such reconsideration, two-thirds of that House shall agree to pass the bill, it shall be sent, together with the objections, to the other House, by which it shall likewise be reconsidered, and if approved by two-thirds of that House, it shall become a law. But in all such cases, the votes of both Houses shall be determined by yeas and nays, and the names of the persons voting for and against the bill shall be entered on the journal of each House respectively. If any bill shall not be returned by the President within ten days (Sundays excepted) after it shall have been presented to him, the same shall be a law in like manner as if he had signed it, unless the Congress, by their adjournment, prevent its return, in which case it shall not be a law.

requisite for the management of said corporation's interests, property, or concerns, and to transact its business. The incorporators shall elect a president and secretary, and shall act as a board of directors until ten thousand dollars ($10,000) of stock have been subscribed and paid in, when and after which time they shall proceed to elect a board of directors, which shall consist of five stockholders, who shall act until their successors are elected according to this charter, to-wit, on the first of January of each year following, and remain in force until their successors are elected.

§ 4. The neglect or fault of said corporation to do any thing or act requiring to be done by said company by this act, or by the General Statutes of this Commonwealth, shall not work a forfeiture of this charter or of the privileges and rights granted hereby.

§ 5. In the event this corporation can not purchase at a reasonable figure natural or artificial gas for use by its subscribers or customers, for the purposes mentioned in the first section hereof, from other companies or corporations making or vending the same, then said company shall have the right, and the same is hereby granted, to dig, bore, and mine for natural gas within said Commonwealth, with the consent of the owners of the land on which said digging, boring, or mining may be done, or to make artificial gas; and when so made or obtained, to deliver the same to its subscribers or customers; and to carry out said rights shall, with the consent of the authorities of the towns, or the General Council of the cities thereof, have the right and authority to open the ground in any highway, street, lane, alley, park, or public place within the limits thereof; and to lay and repair their pipes and mains for conducting and delivering their gas to their subscribers or customers, said company being required to restore the same to as good a condition as before within a reasonable time thereafter, and not to injure any gas or water-pipe or main or sewer laid therein in said towns or cities, and being liable for any damage done by its negligence in so laying, restoring, or repairing as aforesaid. And such authority or power is subject to such reasonable regulations as said authorities or council may make for the protection of life, property, and health of their citizens: *Provided*, That this section shall apply only to Jefferson County and the city of Louisville, and that nothing in this act shall be construed to give said company a right to exercise any of the powers granted in said fifth section outside

AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE LOUISVILLE SAVINGS INVESTMENT ASSOCIATION.

WHEREAS, Experience has demonstrated the beneficial results to the industrious and careful of having a place of investment for their combined earnings and savings; and whereas, it being right and proper to encourage our young men in ways of saving and properly investing their earnings, it is important that such an association should have a legal existence by being incorporated and established by law with perpetual succession, for the better security of those interested, and for the purpose of receiving, holding, and improving such real and personal estate as the said association may become possessed of; therefore.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That there is hereby created and established in the city of Louisville, Kentucky, an investment company, which shall be a body-politic and corporate, by the name of the Louisville Savings Investment Association, and shall have power and authority in that name to contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, answer and defend, in all courts and places as a natural person; and may have and use a common seal and alter and change the same at will; and may regulate its affairs by the establishment of by-laws as hereinafter set forth.

§ 2. The property, business, and affairs of the association created by the first section of this act shall be under the management, government, and control of a board of five directors, which may be
