CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 77

AN ACT RELATING TO THE CLERKS OF THE GENERAL COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE.

§ 1. That the clerk of the Board of Aldermen shall, as soon as practicable after each session, prepare an accurate report of the proceedings of his board, and present the same under his signature to the city printer for publication, and shall keep a journal in a plain, legible hand of the proceedings of his board, and furnish a correct index thereto, and keep a record, and enter therein all ordinances which have passed the General Council and have become operative by the signature of the mayor or otherwise, and furnish a correct index thereto; he shall file and preserve, in bundles of convenient size, properly marked with dates, all ordinances, contracts, apportionments, and bonds; he shall be clerk of the General Council when in joint session, and clerk of the street improvement committee; he shall attest copies of all records or papers in the custody of the clerks of the General Council to be used in courts of law or by the city; he shall make out notices of garnishments of rents for taxes as reported to him by the receiver of city taxes, in accordance with an act to revise and amend the tax laws of the city of Louisville and laws amendatory thereof, and when a claim has passed the General Council and been approved by the mayor, or become valid otherwise, he shall attest the duplicate and deliver the same to the Auditor.

§ 2. That the clerk of the Board of Councilmen shall, as soon as practicable after each session, prepare an accurate report of the proceedings of his board, and present the same under his signature to the city printer for publication; and shall keep a journal in a plain, legible hand of the proceedings of his board, and furnish a correct index thereto; and keep a record and enter therein all resolutions which have passed the General Council and become operative by the signature of the mayor or otherwise, and furnish a correct index thereto; and to furnish to the different departments copies of all such resolutions pertaining to their departments, and keep a record in alphabetical order of each claim and pay-roll that has passed the General Council and received the signature of the mayor, giving the dates it passed each board and the date it was approved by the mayor. He shall file and preserve, in bundles of convenient size, properly marked with dates for each month, all
CONSTITUTION OF KENTUCKY. 227

sentatives have voted for calling a convention, the General Assembly shall, at their next session, pass a law calling a convention, to consist of as many members as there shall be in the House of Representatives and no more, to be chosen on the first Monday in August thereafter, in the same manner and proportion, and at the same places, and possessed of the same qualifications of a qualified elector, by citizens entitled to vote for Representatives, and to meet within three months after their election for the purpose of readopting, amending, or changing this Constitution; but if it shall appear by the vote of either year, as aforesaid, that a majority of all the citizens entitled to vote for Representatives did not vote for calling a convention, a convention shall not then be called. And for the purpose of ascertaining whether a majority of the citizens, entitled to vote for Representatives, did or did not vote for calling a convention, as above, the General Assembly passing the law authorizing such vote shall provide for ascertaining the number of citizens entitled to vote for Representatives within the State.

§ 2. The convention, when assembled, shall judge of the election of its members and decide contested elections, but the General Assembly shall, in calling a convention, provide for taking testimony in such cases, and for issuing a writ of election in case of a tie.

resolutions, claims, and pay-rolls, and shall notify all keepers of taverns and coffee-houses when their licenses have been granted by the General Council, and file said petitions in bundles of convenient size. He shall attest copies of all records or papers in the custody of the clerks of the General Council, to be used in courts of law or by the city; to issue all apportionment warrants against owners of property for grading or paving streets, alleys, or sidewalks, or for digging and walling cisterns, or constructing wells, and keep a record and enter therein all of said warrants issued, and furnish a correct index thereto; to make out all distraint warrants for taxes as reported to him by the receiver of taxes, in accordance with an act to revise and amend the tax laws of the city of Louisville and laws amendatory thereof.

§ 3. That said clerks shall, after each meeting, sign all papers that have passed the General Council, and procure the signature of the presidents of each board to same, and immediately thereafter deliver the same, except the vouchers and pay-rolls, to the city attorney, who shall deliver the same to the mayor of said city after examination. The mayor shall indorse on every paper the time it was presented to him, and the city attorney shall report to the clerk of the Board of Aldermen the time of delivery of the paper by him to the mayor, and the clerk shall record such report.

§ 4. The said clerks shall give bond, to be approved by the General Council, for the faithful performance of their duties.

§ 5. The salaries of said clerks shall be fifteen hundred dollars, and shall not exceed sixteen hundred dollars each per annum, to be paid in monthly installments.

§ 6. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved April 22, 1886.

§ 5. That all men have a natural and indefeasible right to worship Almighty God according to the dictates of their own consciences; that no man shall be compelled to attend, erect, or support any place of worship, or to maintain any ministry against his consent; that no human authority ought, in any case whatever, to control or interfere with the rights of conscience; and that no preference shall ever be given, by law, to any religious societies or modes of worship.

§ 6. That the civil rights, privileges, or capacities of any citizen shall in nowise be diminished or enlarged on account of his religion.

§ 7. That all elections shall be free and equal.

§ 8. That the ancient mode of trial by jury shall be held sacred, and the right thereof remain inviolate, subject to such modifications as may be authorized by this Constitution.

§ 9. That printing presses shall be free to every person who undertakes to examine the proceedings of the General Assembly, or any branch of government; and no law shall ever be made to restrain the right thereof. The free communication of thoughts and opinions is one of the invaluable rights of man, and every citizen may freely speak, write, and print on any subject, being responsible for the abuse of that liberty.

§ 10. In prosecutions for the publication of papers investigating the official conduct of officers, or men in a public capacity, or where the matter published is proper for public information, the truth thereof may be given in evidence; and in all indictments for libels, the jury shall have a right to determine the law and the facts under the direction of the court, as in other cases.

AN ACT IN RELATION TO AND FOR THE CONDITIONAL REPEAL OF AN ACT, ENTITLED "AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE NOW EXISTING LOUISVILLE GAS COMPANY, AND TO GRANT IT A NEW CHARTER," APPROVED APRIL 30, 1884.

§ 1. That an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the now existing Louisville Gas Company, and to grant it a new charter," approved April 30, 1884, be, and the same is hereby, repealed, unless the said company shall release, abandon, and disclaim any and all exclusive rights and privileges to erect gas works, and to make, vend, and supply gas in Louisville, claimed by it through any act of the General Assembly, municipal ordinance, or resolution, or through any contract of any character, other than the exclusive right and privilege to make and vend that kind of gas known as coal gas in the city of Louisville.

§ 2. Said relinquishment shall be made by deed regularly executed by the president of said company to the Commonwealth of Kentucky, and acknowledged before the clerk of the county court of Jefferson County, and recorded by him within thirty days from the passage of this act; and when this shall have been done, the repeal provided for herein shall cease, and the said act of thirtieth April, 1884, shall remain in full force and effect.

§ 3. Should said deed of covenant named herein be, within the time prescribed, executed, acknowledged, and delivered to said clerk, he shall record it as other deeds are recorded, and transmit the original to the Secretary of State of this Commonwealth for safekeeping. Copies of said deed, when recorded, shall be evidence in any court of this Commonwealth.

§ 4. This act shall take effect from its passage.

[Became a law without the approval of the Governor, April 28, 1886.]

§ 13. That no person shall, for any indictable offense, be proceeded against criminally by information, except in cases arising in the land or naval forces, or in the militia when in actual service, in time of war or public danger, or by leave of the court, for oppression or misdemeanor in office.

§ 14. No person shall, for the same offense, be twice put in jeopardy of his life or limb; nor shall any man's property be taken or applied to public use without the consent of his representatives, and without just compensation being previously made to him.

§ 15. That all courts shall be open, and every person, for an injury done him in his lands, goods, person, or reputation, shall have remedy by the due course of law, and right and justice administered without sale, denial, or delay.

§ 16. That no power of suspending laws shall be exercised, unless by the General Assembly, or its authority.

§ 17. That excessive bail shall not be required, nor excessive fines imposed, nor cruel punishments inflicted.

That all prisoners shall be bailable by sufficient securities, unless for capital offenses, when the proof is evident or presumption great; and the privilege of the writ of *habeas corpus* shall not be suspended, unless when, in cases of rebellion or invasion, the public safety may require it.

80 CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS.

AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE NATIONAL RAILROAD AGENTS' ASSOCIATION.

WHEREAS, Certain railroad station agents have associated together and organized the National Railroad Agents' Association; and whereas, a meeting was held in Louisville, Kentucky, June 10, 1885, at which a constitution was adopted and officers and an executive board elected; and whereas, said officers and board desire to procure an act of incorporation whereby they and their successors in office shall be made a body-corporate under the name of National Railroad Agents' Association; therefore,

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That J. C. Downing, George Petry, G. L Spink, W. K. Jamison, Brent Arnold, B. W. Briggs, C. B. Cook, J. E. Brawner, J. L. Tefft, and J. G. Barry, and their successors and assigns, be, and they are hereby, created a body-corporate, under the name of National Railroad Agents' Association, and by that name have perpetual succession, and may contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, and may acquire and hold property, real, personal, or mixed, and transact business in its corporate name; and may have and use a common seal, and alter the same at pleasure, and do any and all lawful acts necessary to carry out the objects for which said association is organized.

§ 2. The particular object and business of the said association shall be to render the railroad companies the best standard of agents by improving the quality of their service; also to assist worthy members in obtaining employment; to guarantee the bonds or undertakings of members; to establish a fund, by assessments or otherwise, which shall be collected, held, and used for the benefit of members in case of accident, sickness, or being out of employment, and for the benefit of families of deceased members, or their heirs, or such persons as such deceased members may, while living, direct, and to promote any other worthy objects that may from time to time be introduced.

§ 3. The affairs of said corporation shall be under the control and management of a board of managers consisting of ten persons, one of whom shall be president and another vice president; a ma-

except during the life of the offender, forfeiture of estate to the Commonwealth.

§ 23. That the estates of such persons as shall destroy their own lives shall descend or vest as in case of natural death; and if any person shall be killed by casualty, there shall be no forfeiture by reason thereof.

§ 24. That the citizens have a right, in a peaceable manner, to assemble together for their common good, and to apply to those invested with the powers of government for redress of grievances, or other proper purposes, by petition, address, or remonstrance.

§ 25. That the rights of the citizens to bear arms in defense of themselves and the State shall not be questioned; but the General Assembly may pass laws to prevent persons from carrying concealed arms.

§ 26. That no standing army shall, in time of peace, be kept up, without the consent of the General Assembly; and the military shall, in all cases and at all times, be in strict subordination to the civil power.

§ 27. That no soldier shall, in time of peace, be quartered in any house, without the consent of the owner, nor in time of war, but in a manner to be prescribed by law.

§ 28. That the General Assembly shall not grant any title of nobility or hereditary distinction, nor create any office, the appointment to which shall be for a longer time than for a term of years.

§ 29. That emigration from the State shall not be prohibited.

§ 30. To guard against transgressions of the high powers which we have delegated, WE DECLARE, that everything in this article is excepted out of the general powers of government, and shall forever remain inviolate; and that all laws contrary thereto, or contrary to this Constitution, shall be void.

jority of the board shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of business; and the persons named in the first section of this act as incorporators shall be, and they are hereby, constituted the first board of managers for said corporation. The board shall have full power to fill vacancies. A majority of the board of managers shall reside in or be in easy access to the principal place of business of said association. The persons herein named as incorporators, or a majority of them, may constitute a board to do business, and may proceed at pleasure to organize and may receive as members any railroad agent or his assistant, in good standing, and such other persons as may be recommended by a member in good standing; but no member shall be entitled to share in the relief or benefit funds contemplated herein until he shall have passed a satisfactory medical examination and be shown to be in good health, or until he shall have complied with all by-laws, rules, or regulations of the association in regard to members or their admission into this association or its benefits.

§ 4. It shall be lawful for the corporation to create, hold, and disburse a beneficiary fund or funds for the relief of members and their families, and to establish a benefit fund, from which, on satisfactory evidence of the death of a member who has complied with the legal requirements of the association, a sum, to be fixed by the board of managers or the association, shall be paid to the family or heirs of such member, or be paid in such manner or to such person as said member may have directed. The manner of raising and disbursing such relief or benefit fund or funds shall be controlled and regulated by such by-laws, rules, or regulations of the said corporation as it may be necessary to adopt; and all of said funds shall be exempt from execution, and not liable to seizure or appropriation by any legal or equitable process for any debt or debts of the living or deceased members of said association.

§ 5. The said corporation may also establish a guarantee department, and by a system of membership fees, dues, and assessments upon members applying for membership in or belonging to said department, may provide for all expenses and liabilities incurred in organizing or operating said department. Upon the organization of such department as herein provided, the corporation may execute bonds of suretyship or guaranty for members of said department of said association, and may, upon the legal requirements of

§ 2. The oaths of office herein directed to be taken may be administered by any judge or justice of the peace, until the General Assembly shall otherwise direct.

§ 3. No office shall be superseded by the adoption of this Constitution, but the laws of the State relative to the duties of the several officers, legislative, executive, judicial, and military, shall remain in full force, though the same be contrary to this Constitution, and the several duties shall be performed by the respective officers of the State according to the existing laws, until the organization of the government, as provided for under this Constitution, and the entering into office of the officers to be elected or appointed under said government, and no longer.

§ 4. It shall be the duty of the General Assembly which shall convene in the year 1850 to make an apportionment of the representation of this State, upon the principle set forth in this Constitution; and until the first apportionment shall be made as herein directed, the apportionment of Senators and Representatives among the several districts and counties in this State shall remain as at present fixed by law: *Provided*, That on the first Monday in August, 1850, all Senators shall go out of office, and on that day an election for Senators and Representatives shall be held throughout the State, and those then elected shall hold their offices for one year, and no longer: *Provided, further*, That at the elections to be held in the year 1850, that provision in this Constitution which requires voters to vote in the precinct within which they reside shall not apply.

§ 5. All recognizances heretofore taken, or which may be taken before the organization of the judicial department under this Constitution, shall remain as valid as though this Constitution had not been adopted, and may be prosecuted in the name of the Commonwealth. All criminal prosecutions and penal actions which have arisen, or may arise before the reorganization of the judicial department under this Constitution, may be prosecuted to judgment and execution in the name of the Commonwealth.

The guarantee department shall constitute a separate department of the association, and all the business of said department, together with all the funds, securities, investments, or property of said corporation pertaining or belonging to said department, shall be kept separate and apart from the other business or funds of the association. The association shall, by proper by-laws, fix the rates, terms, and conditions upon which the association will become surety or guarantor, as well as the length of time or amount for which the corporation will become liable on bonds or obligations of suretyship or guaranty, and also shall, by such by-laws, fix the amount each member of said department shall be assessed upon any bond executed for such member, and the amount members of the department shall be assessed to meet the current expenses or any liability of said corporation on any of the undertakings or bonds contemplated and authorized by this act. The amount of any and all assessments on members shall be regulated by the amount of the liability assumed by the corporation on the respective bonds of members; that is, where a member is assessed upon a bond being executed for such member, the amount paid shall be in proportion to the amount of such bond, and the ratio of assessments on members to meet liabilities shall be fixed and controlled by the amount of the respective bonds executed for members; except, when the association is not liable on any bond for a member of said department, such member shall pay a minimum assessment, to be fixed by the association.

It also specifies the process for admitting new members to this department, requiring a satisfactory examination of moral character, integrity, and standing, and payment of a membership fee.

CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 83

becoming a member thereof shall pay a membership fee of not less than five dollars ($5); and when as much as five thousand dollars shall have been subscribed to the permanent fund contemplated herein, or when one thousand members shall have been admitted, the managers may organize the guarantee department contemplated herein. Subject to the limitations named herein, the sums received as membership fees and annual dues in said guarantee department shall form a permanent fund, which shall be invested in good securities and kept separate and apart from the other investments of said guarantee department or of said corporation, and such investments shall be specially and primarily liable for the obligations of said corporation as surety on the bonds of members of said department as authorized herein; and such investment shall not be liable for any other debt or liability of said corporation until all of its liabilities on such undertakings or obligations shall have first been met and discharged. The membership fees shall constitute the permanent fund of said department of said association, and shall not be used for any other purpose than as specified above: *Provided*, That when said fund shall reach seventy-five thousand dollars, the managers may, in their discretion, apply any excess or surplus over said sum to defraying expenses or liquidating any liability or obligation of the said guarantee department of the said corporation. All of the obligations or indebtedness of said guarantee department, of every kind and character, shall first be discharged before any of its funds shall be in any manner liable for any other debt or obligation of said corporation.

§ 8. The officers of the association shall be president, vice-president, secretary, treasurer, and such other officers, agents, or employes as may be provided for herein, or such as the managers may deem necessary and convenient for the proper prosecution of the business of the association. The office of secretary and treasurer may be held by the same person. The board of managers and the officers elected shall hold their office for one year and until their successors are elected and qualified. The election of a new board of managers shall take place within one year after the organization of the association under this incorporation, and until such election the incorporators are to constitute the board of managers, and J. C. Downing shall be president, and George Petry vice-president, and G. L. Spink secretary and treasurer.

§ 9. The board of managers may fix the salaries and compensation of officers and employes, and prescribe in the by-laws the duties of officers and the amount and condition of such bonds as may be required of them.

§ 10. The board of managers shall make all by-laws necessary for conducting the business of the corporation. The by-laws shall regulate the time and manner of holding all regular or called meetings of the board or of the association, and shall fix the membership fees and assessments, and the rules, principles, or terms on which members may be admitted; and also the terms upon which memberships or benefits may be forfeited or members expelled; and may make such other regulations as may be necessary to the proper conduct or management of the business of the association or any of its departments.

§ 11. The principal place of business of the association shall be Louisville, Kentucky; but the corporation may, under such rules and regulations as shall be adopted, constitute and establish offices, branches, sections, or local boards of the association at other places.

§ 12. The president and managers shall cause to be made annually a full report of the business of the association, subject to the inspection of its members.

§ 13. The private property of the members shall be exempt from the corporate debts or liabilities.

§ 14. That so much of the act of April 19, 1884, relating to Assessment Insurance Companies of other States, as may be applicable to this corporation, shall be held and deemed to apply thereto, except so much thereof as taxes the premium receipts of such corporations.

§ 15. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved April 28, 1886.

The capital stock of the said corporation shall be ($125,000) one hundred and twenty-five thousand dollars, divided into shares of one hundred dollars each; and all stock subscribed for shall be paid in at par into the treasury of the company. The above named incorporators, or their survivors, or a majority of them, shall, at such times and places as they may determine, open books for subscription to such capital stock.

§ 3. The affairs of the said corporation shall be managed by a board of not less than five nor more than seven directors, who shall be elected by the stockholders to serve for one year, and until their successors shall be elected. The above named incorporators shall act as a board of directors until the stockholders shall elect a board; and whenever the above named capital shall have been subscribed, or sooner, if the said incorporators shall so determine, they shall call a meeting of the subscribers or stockholders, to be held at a fixed time and place, for the election of a board of directors and the transaction of other business. The board shall elect one of their number to be president, and may select or provide for the election or appointment of such subordinate officers as they may deem expedient. At all meetings of the stockholders each share of stock shall be entitled to one vote, which may be cast in person or by proxy.

§ 4. The said company shall have authority to enter into and perform any contract or contracts with any company authorized to build a railroad in the State of Kentucky, for the location, construction, or equipment of the whole or any part of such railroad; and in order to make and carry out the same may acquire, own, and dispose of any property, real or personal, which it may deem necessary therefor, and do all things needful or proper for the location, construction, and equipment of any such railroad; and may contract for and receive in payment from any such company, and dispose of the bonds, choses in action, or shares of the capital stock of such company.

# RULES OF GOVERNMENT

OF THE

# BOARD OF ALDERMEN.

1. The stated meetings of the Board of Aldermen shall be held in their chamber, corner of Sixth and Jefferson streets, every thirty days, and at such other times as may be designated by adjournment, to be opened at such hour as the board may from time to time prescribe; and seven members shall constitute a quorum to do business.

2. The president shall take the chair at the time appointed for the board to meet, and shall call the members to order, and on the appearance of a quorum shall cause the journal of the preceding meeting to be read, and, no objections being made, it shall be considered approved, when the board may proceed to business.

3. In case the president shall not attend, the clerk, on the appearance of a quorum, shall call the board to order, when a president *pro tem.* shall be appointed by the board for that meeting, or until the appearance of the president.

4. It shall be the duty of the president to preserve order; and if any member transgress the rules of the board, the president shall, or any member may, call him to order, in which case the member called to order shall at once take his seat, unless permitted to explain. The president may speak to points of order in preference to other members. He shall decide all questions of order, subject to an appeal to the board.

5. The president shall vote upon all questions, and when the yeas and nays are called the president shall first be called, and then the members by wards, beginning at the upper ward; and if the vote be equally divided, the question shall be lost.

AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE LOUISVILLE DETECTIVE AGENCY.

§ 1. That Gus Rosenberg, Thomas Shepard, and Wm. Coffey be, and they are hereby, incorporated, under the name of the Louisville Detective Agency.
§ 2. Said corporation shall exist for the period of twenty-five years, and during that time shall have the right to sue and be sued, contract and be contracted with, and have all the powers incidental to corporate bodies.
§ 3. The business of said corporation shall be the searching for and arresting criminals, ferreting out crimes, and other detective work of a general nature, and pertaining to the administration of law in all or any of its branches.
§ 4. The principal office of said corporation shall be in the city of Louisville, Kentucky; but said corporation shall have power to arrest criminals in any part of the State of Kentucky: *Provided, however*, They shall be governed in all respects by the laws for such purpose in all such cases made and provided.
§ 5. The original incorporators shall serve as directors for said corporation for the term of one year, and thereafter a board of three directors shall be elected by the stockholders on the first Tuesday in May of each year, who shall serve until their successors are elected and installed.
§ 6. The officers of the corporation shall be a president, secretary and treasurer; they shall be elected by the board of directors at their first meeting after their organization, and shall hold their offices for the term of one year and until their successors are duly elected and qualified.
§ 7. The private property of the stockholders shall be exempt from the corporate debts.
§ 8. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved May 1, 1886.

6. Whenever the president may wish to leave the chair he shall have power to substitute a member in his place: *Provided*, The substitution shall not continue beyond the day on which it is made.
7. Every member present when a question is put shall vote, unless the board shall, for special reasons, excuse him.
8. Every motion, resolution, or proposition shall, if the president or any member of the board require it, be reduced to writing.
9. No ordinance shall be sent from this board for concurrence on the same day on which it passed this board, nor shall any ordinance sent to this board from the other board for concurrence be acted upon to its final passage the same day it passed the other board.
10. When a motion is made and seconded it shall be deemed to be in possession of the board, and shall be stated by the president, or if in writing may be read by the mover, or handed to the clerk to be read by him previous to debate.
11. When a question is under debate no motion shall be received, except to adjourn, to lay on the table, for the previous question, to postpone to a certain time, to commit, to amend, or to postpone indefinitely. These several motions shall have precedence in the order in which they are enumerated.
12. A motion for amendment, until it is decided, shall preclude all other amendments, but shall not preclude members from reading substitutes or amendments for the information of the board previous to the decision on the preceding amendment, and every amendment or substitute shall be reduced to writing. But no amendment which tends to destroy the general sense of any clause or motion shall be admitted; nor shall a motion or proposition different from that under consideration be admitted as an amendment.
13. A motion or resolution may be withdrawn at any time before action is had on it, by consent of the board.
14. All questions to be put in form, "All who are of the opinion that (as the question may be) say *aye*; those of a contrary opinion, *no*;" and in doubtful cases the president may direct or any member may call for a division.
15. When a member is about to speak he shall rise and respectfully address himself to the president, and the president shall pronounce the ward represented by the member entitled to speak;

AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE LOUISVILLE & PORTSMOUTH RAILWAY COMPANY.

§ 1. That the persons who shall become stockholders pursuant to this act in the company hereby incorporated, and their successors, shall be, and are hereby, made a body-politic and corporate, in perpetuity, under the name and style of the Louisville & Portsmouth Railway Company, with power to construct, use, maintain, and operate a railway, with one or more tracks, and such appendages as may be deemed necessary for the use of the same between the points, viz.: Louisville, Kentucky, and Portsmouth, Ohio, and said company shall be capable in law of purchasing, holding, leasing, selling, and conveying any real and personal estate whatever, of contracting and being contracted with, suing and being sued, and having a common seal, which they may alter or renew at pleasure, and have and enjoy and exercise all the rights, powers, and privileges which corporations may lawfully have.

§ 2. That James M. Thomas, James W. Ferguson, and Wm. Tarr, of the county of Bourbon, are hereby appointed commissioners, with power, in case of death of either, to appoint their successor, under the direction of any two of whom subscriptions may be received to the capital stock of said railway company; and they may cause books to be opened at such time and place as they may direct, for the purpose of receiving such subscriptions, after having given notice in such manner as they may appoint; and they may continue to take subscriptions for such time as they may deem expedient.

§ 3. That the capital stock of the said railway company shall be five millions of dollars, in shares of one hundred dollars each, which may be subscribed for by any individual or corporation; and as soon as five thousand shares of said capital stock shall have been subscribed, the subscribers of said stock, their successors and assigns, shall be, and are hereby declared to be, incorporated into a company by the name of the Louisville & Portsmouth Railway Company.

§ 4. That at every subscription of stock there shall be paid to the commissioners the sum of one dollar for every share subscribed, and the residue shall be paid in such installments and at such times as may be required by the board of directors of the said railway

RULES OF GOVERNMENT OF BOARD OF ALDERMEN. 237

and no member shall be allowed to speak except from his own desk, and standing.

16. No member shall speak more than twice on any question, nor longer than ten minutes each time, without leave of the board, nor more than once until every member of the board choosing to speak shall have spoken.

17. No member shall absent himself without permission from the president.

18. A motion to adjourn shall always be in order, unless the board is engaged in voting or a member speaking, and shall be decided without debate.

19. The previous question shall be put in these words, "Shall the main question be now put?" and it shall be admitted on demand of two members, and until decided shall preclude all debate or amendment of the main question.

20. Any member may call for a division of the question when the same shall admit thereof.

21. The first reading of an ordinance shall be for information, and if objection be made to it, the question shall be, "Shall the proposition be rejected?" If no objection be made, or the question to reject be lost, the ordinance proposed shall go to a second reading without further question.

22. Upon a second reading of an ordinance, the president shall state it ready for commitment, amendment, or postponement. If no proposition is made to commit, amend, or postpone, the president shall put the question of passage: "As many as are of opinion that the ordinance shall now pass say yea; contrary, no."

23. An ordinance, after commitment or report thereof, may be recommitted at any time previous to its final passage.

24. The yeas and nays shall be taken and recorded, upon any question before the board, upon a call of any one member, and any member shall have the right to dissent from and protest against any ordinance, resolution, or order of the board.

25. Every ordinance shall be introduced by leave, or by order of the board, or report of a committee; and no ordinance or resolution shall be passed or repealed, or contract or appropriation of money made, or for the payment of salaries or money on account of contract adopted, or carrying out the powers of an ordinance, except upon report from the proper committee, and seven members voting in the affirmative.

238 RULES OF GOVERNMENT OF BOARD OF ALDERMEN.

26. All ordinances appropriating money shall be investigated by the Finance Committee before their passage, and in case they are presented without such investigation they shall be referred without debate to the Finance Committee for consideration.
27. In filling blanks the largest sum and the longest time shall be put first.
28. All committees shall be appointed by the president unless otherwise directed by the board.
29. At the meetings of the board the business shall be taken up in the following order, to-wit:
    (1) Communications from city officials.
    (2) Petitions and remonstrances.
    (3) New claims against the city.
    (4) Reports from standing committees.
    (5) Reports from special committees.
    (6) Unfinished business.
    (7) New business and business from the Board of Councilmen.
No business shall be transacted during the call of committees unless by a vote of eight members of the board.
30. No personalities or reflections injurious to the feelings or harmony of the board or any member thereof shall be tolerated, and the member indulging in any such personalities shall be called to order by the president or any member; and no member shall absent himself from the board while in session without leave from the president.
31. Whenever the doors are directed to be closed all persons except members and clerk and sergeant-at-arms shall retire.
32. The president nor any member of the board shall be named in debate or during the session of the board by name, but shall be designated by the ward such member represents.
33. Every member on presenting a paper to the chair shall first state its general purport.
34. This board may at any time resolve itself into a Committee of the Whole on the state of the city.
35. In forming a Committee of the Whole the president shall leave the chair, and the chairman to preside in said committee shall be appointed by the president.
36. All amendments made to the original proposition in Committee of the Whole shall be incorporated with it and so reported.

§ 10. It shall be lawful for the said company to unite their said road with any other railway, either in or out of the State of Kentucky, and it shall in like manner be lawful for any other railroad company, now or thereafter incorporated, to unite with this road upon such terms and conditions as may be agreed upon by and between the directors of the respective companies.

§ 2. The Beargrass Railway Company is hereby granted the right to build, maintain, and operate one or more lines of railway in the territory, and upon the terms and conditions mentioned in the charter or amendments of the Jefferson Railway Company; and so much of the exclusive privilege in the charter of the Jefferson Railway Company as would prevent or interfere with the Beargrass Railway Company exercising the rights herein granted is hereby repealed, so far as may be necessary for the said companies to enjoy equal rights.

§ 3. That hereafter said Central Passenger Railroad Company and said Beargrass Railway Company may, with the consent of the General Council of the city of Louisville, given by ordinance or resolution, consolidate and be merged into one corporation, under the name of the Central Passenger Railroad Company, upon such terms and conditions as may be agreed upon between said city and company; and shall thereupon have perpetual succession, and the right to sue and be sued by such name, and exercise all the powers and privileges incident to such corporation; and all rights, privileges, and immunities belonging or appertaining to either one of said companies shall thereafter belong and appertain to said consolidated company.

§ 4. That section ten of the Jefferson Railway Company's charter, prohibiting the riding, driving, or leading of any animal or vehicle along the roadway of said company, is hereby repealed.

§ 5. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved May 7, 1886.

§ 1. There is hereby established a public landing in the county of Jefferson, on the Ohio River, beginning at a point on the said Ohio River and the Fountain Ferry Road, and extending along the
# RULES OF GOVERNMENT

OF THE

# BOARD OF COUNCILMEN.

1. The president with three members of the Board of Councilmen shall be a sufficient number to adjourn; five to call a council, and send for absent members, and make an order for their censure, and impose a fine; and a majority of said board to proceed to business.

2. The president shall take the chair at every meeting at the hour to which the Council had adjourned at the preceding meeting. He shall immediately call the members to order, and on the appearance of a quorum he shall cause the journal of the preceding meeting to be read, and when approved it shall at once be signed by the president, in the presence of the Council, before proceeding with any other business.

3. He shall preserve order and decorum; may speak to points of order in preference to other members without rising from his chair for that purpose. He shall decide questions of order, subject to an appeal to the Council on the request of two members.

4. Business of the Council shall be—
    (1) Communications from the mayor and other city officers.
    (2) Petitions, to be called by wards, commencing at the First and Twelfth wards alternately, until all the wards are called.
    (3) Reports of standing committees in the order in which they stand.
    (4) Reports from select committees.
    (5) Motions and resolutions by wards.
    (6) Introduction of ordinances by wards.

AN ACT CONCERNING LICENSE TAXES IN THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE.

§ 1. That an act to revise and amend the tax laws of the city of Louisville, approved May 12, 1884, be, and it is hereby, amended as follows: In article 1 of said act, section 9, after the words "the required license," shall read thus: For every confectioner, hawker, huckster, or peddler, not less than five dollars nor more than one hundred dollars. Any other merchants or dealers (except as hereinafter provided for), not less than five dollars nor more than twenty-five hundred dollars. In grading the license, the General Council shall impose a tax of not less than one dollar on every thousand dollars of yearly sales at wholesale; of all other commodities, except grain, cotton, and provisions, not less than fifty cents on every thousand dollars of yearly sales at wholesale of these articles, and of not less than two dollars on every thousand dollars of yearly sales at retail; and shall collect the license tax within the above limits at the end of the year for the excess of sales over the estimate made at the time of issuing the license. The sum of twenty-one hundred dollars shall, on the first of every month, beginning on the first of June, 1884, be paid by the Sinking Fund Commissioners into the city treasury for the benefit of the school fund, in lieu of the former school tax on merchandise. 5. If the call of the committees is not gone through at any meeting, it shall be the duty of the president to commence where he left off at the previous meeting, until all the committees are called in their regular order.

6. Questions shall be distinctly put in this form, viz.: "All you who are of opinion that (as the question may be) say aye; you of the contrary opinion say no."

7. If the president doubt, or if a division be called for, the Council shall divide. Those in the affirmative of the question shall first rise from their seats, and afterward those in the negative. The president shall thereupon decide, subject to an appeal to the Council.

8. The president shall vote upon all questions, and when the yeas and nays are called the president shall first be called; and if the Council be equally divided, the question shall be lost.

9. The president shall have the right to name any member to perform the duties of the chair; but such substitution shall not extend beyond an adjournment. He may participate in debate when out of the chair.

10. The Council in the absence of the president may appoint some one of its members president *pro tem.* during said absence.

11. The president shall appoint the following standing committees, viz.: A Committee on Finance; a Committee on Revision; a Committee on Railroads; a Committee on Streets for the Eastern District, and also one for the Western District; a Committee on Taverns and Groceries for the Eastern District, and also one for the Western District; a Committee on Gas and Water; a Committee on Fire Department; a Committee on Police; a Committee on Wharf; a Committee on Contracts; a Committee on Public Charities; a Committee on Court-house and City Buildings; a Committee on Emigration; a Committee on Printing; a Committee on Grievances; a Committee on Elections and Bonds; a Committee on Cemeteries; a Committee on Assessments; a Committee on Health; a Committee on Sewers for the Eastern District, and also one for the Western District; a Committee on Street Improvement; a Committee on Rules; a Committee on Charter Amendments. No committee shall sit during the session of the Council, except the Committee on Revision, without the leave of the Council.

RULES OF GOVERNMENT OF BOARD OF COUNCILMEN. 243

DEBATE.

12. When a member is about to speak in debate, or deliver any matter to the Council, he shall rise and respectfully address himself to Mr. President.

13. If any member, in speaking or otherwise, transgress the rules of the Council, the president shall, or any member may, call to order such member, and any member so called to order shall immediately sit down, unless permitted to explain; and the Council, if appealed to, shall decide on the case without debate. If the decision shall be in favor of the member called to order, he shall be at liberty to proceed; if against him, and the case require it, he shall be liable to the censure of the Council.

14. When two or more members rise at the same time to speak, the president shall name the first to speak.

15. No member shall speak more than twice on the same question without leave of the Council, nor more than once until every member in the Council choosing to speak shall have spoken; and no member shall speak more than five minutes without leave of the Council.

16. While the president is putting a question or addressing the Council none shall walk across or out of the Council; neither, in such case, when a member is speaking, shall any entertain private discourse, nor while a member is speaking shall any pass between him and the president.

17. Every member who shall be in the Council when a question is put shall vote on one side or the other, unless the Council for special cause shall excuse him.

18. When a motion is made and seconded it shall be stated by the president, or, being in writing, shall be handed to the chair, and read aloud by the clerk. Every motion shall be reduced to writing if the president or any member desire it.

19. After a motion is stated by the president or read by the clerk it shall be considered in possession of the Council; but may be withdrawn at any time before a decision or amendments by the mover thereof.

20. When a question is under debate no motion shall be received, unless to amend it, commit it, or postpone it, for the previous question, or to adjourn, or to lay on the table.

architects, dentists, and lawyers and brewers, not herein named, the General Council may, by ordinance, designate and fix the rate or rates between five dollars and twenty-five hundred dollars, and cause a license therefor to be taken as herein provided. Every business, profession, or craft herein provided to be licensed the General Council may grade and classify, and fix the rate of license at or within the maximum and minimum herein named. No license shall be for a longer period than one year, but may be for a shorter term, in the discretion of the General Council, and the charge therefor shall be paid into the Sinking Fund before granting the license. The resident agent of non-resident proprietors shall be civilly responsible for the license tax and criminally responsible for carrying on business without license, in like manner as if they were proprietors. The unexpired term of a license may be transferred by the holder, with the assent of the General Council, expressed by ordinance, on payment of five per cent. of the original cost. The secretary and treasurer of the Sinking Fund shall, from the oath of the applicant or other evidence, recommend to the General Council the grade in which the applicant shall be classed under the ordinance of said city, when the application is to license a tavern, coffee-house, or other place where spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors are sold. In granting all other licenses, the secretary and treasurer of the Sinking Fund shall, from the oath of the applicant or other evidence, ascertain the grade in which such applicant should be licensed; but such applicant shall have the right, within ten days, to appeal to the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund from the action of said secretary and treasurer, and the commissioners shall have power to determine in which grade the applicant shall be placed. From the words, "The council may also levy, and so forth," the ninth section shall remain unchanged.

244 RULES OF GOVERNMENT OF BOARD OF COUNCILMEN.

21. A motion to adjourn shall always be in order, unless the Council is engaged in voting, and shall be decided without debate.
22. The previous question being moved and seconded, the question from the chair shall be, "Shall the main question be now put?" and if the nays prevail, the main question shall not be then put. But a refusal to sustain the previous question shall not bar the Council from proceeding forthwith to the consideration of the subject. The effect of the previous question shall be to put an end to all debate, bringing the Council to a direct vote upon the amendments reported by a committee, if any, then upon pending amendments, and then upon the main question.
23. Any member may call for the division of a question when the sense will admit of it.
24. A motion for amendment, until it is decided, shall preclude all other amendments of the main question.
25. Motions and reports may be committed at the pleasure of the Council.
26. No motion or proposition on a subject different from that under consideration shall be admitted under color of an amendment.
27. In all cases of elections there shall be a previous nomination by some member of the Council.
28. If a question pending be lost by adjournment of the Council, and revived on the succeeding meeting, no member who has spoken twice at the previous meeting on said question shall speak on said question again without leave.
29. Petitions, memorials, and other papers addressed to the Council may be presented by any member in his place, who shall briefly state to the Council the contents thereof, which may be received, read, and referred on the same day to the appropriate committee.
30. On a call of the Council for the yeas and nays on any question pending, the names of the members shall be called alphabetically, which call may be made at any time when a division or count may be had.
31. On the call of the Council the door shall not be shut against any member until his name shall be twice called, and then the absentees shall be noted down by the clerk.
32. No member while in debate shall name any other member present.

be collected and paid over to said Louisville Industrial School of
Reform, and the same shall be used and expended in running and
operating the same.

§ 3. That so much of said act of March 9, 1854, and the amend-
ments thereto, as fixes the term for the confinement and retention
of females in said institution, be, and is, so amended as to read,
that when any female has been placed in said institution she shall
there remain until she arrives at twenty-one (21) years of age, unless
sooner discharged.

§ 4. The managers of said Louisville Industrial School of Re-
form shall have the power to grant to any of its inmates a ticket of
leave to go to any place in or out of the State of Kentucky.

§ 5. This act shall be in force and effect from and after its pas-
sage; and all acts or parts of acts in conflict herewith are hereby
repealed.

Approved May 13, 1886.

RULES OF GOVERNMENT OF BOARD OF COUNCILMEN. 245

33. On motion to proceed or dispense with the orders of the day, to dispense with any rule of the Council, to take up an ordinance or resolution out of its regular order, shall require a majority of two-thirds of the members elect; to commit or recommit shall be propounded without debate. A motion to reconsider a vote shall not be made after the next meeting of the Council at which such vote was adopted.

34. The chairman of a committee to whom is referred any subject for investigation shall submit the report of the committee at the next regular meeting of the Council, or within a reasonable time thereafter; and all reports, whether in favor of or against any matter referred, shall be in writing if required by the president; and in all cases where the report is against the question referred the reasons for said report shall be given, but this rule shall not interfere with the right to move a reconsideration.

35. All propositions, ordinances, or resolutions which have once been rejected by the Council shall not be again introduced, unless by special leave granted by a vote of a majority of the members elect.

ORDINANCES.

36. Every ordinance shall be read at two meetings of the Council, unless two-thirds of the members elect shall vote for the dispensation of this rule.

37. The first reading of an ordinance shall be for information; unless the rules be suspended, that it may have its second reading, the ordinance shall be referred to its appropriate committee.

38. Upon the second reading of the ordinance the president shall state that it is ready for amendment or recommitment, and if there is no motion to amend or recommit, the question shall be on its passage.

39. Upon the passage of any ordinance appropriating moneys, or for the paving or grading of any street, alley, or the digging or walling of any well or cistern in said city, the yeas and nays shall be taken and recorded in full.

AMENDING RULES.

40. No standing rule or order of the Council shall be rescinded or changed, or new rules introduced, unless notice of the motion thereof had been given at the preceding meeting.

96 CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS.

AN ACT, ENTITLED "AN ACT TO AMEND THE CHARTER OF THE BANK OF LOUISVILLE."

§ 1. That the directors of the Bank of Louisville are authorized to elect from their number a vice-president, who shall, in case of the absence, resignation, or death of the president, discharge the duties of president of said bank until the return of the president or the vacancy be filled. He shall be elected at the same time the president is elected, and shall hold his office for the same term, except that the directors of said bank may elect a vice-president, to serve until the next annual election.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved May 12, 1886.

AN ACT RELATING TO THE PUBLICATION OF THE CHARTER AND ORDINANCES OF THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE.

§ 1. That it shall be the duty of clerk of the Board of Aldermen of the city of Louisville, at the beginning of each year, commencing with the year 1886, to collect, compile, index, and cause to be published, in book form, all ordinances of a general nature in force at the close of the preceding year, and all general ordinances not theretofore so published, together with all amendments to the city charter or acts relating to the Louisville city government; and said clerk shall receive for such service such sum as the General Council may provide.

§ 2. All acts in conflict herewith are hereby repealed; and this act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved May 12, 1886.

246 RULES OF GOVERNMENT OF BOARD OF COUNCILMEN.

41. No standing rule or order of the Council shall be dispensed with unless two-thirds of the members elect concur therein.
42. No persons except members, city officers, or reporters for the city press, shall be admitted within the bar of the council chamber, unless permitted by a vote of the Council. It shall be the *imperative* duty of the sergeant-at-arms to strictly enforce this rule.
43. No member shall leave the Council without special leave being granted.
44. That the Council shall adjourn at 10½ o'clock P. M., motions and resolutions to the contrary notwithstanding.

COMMITTEE OF THE WHOLE.

45. The Council may at any time resolve itself into a Committee of the Whole on the state of the city.
46. In forming a Committee of the Whole the president shall leave the chair, and a chairman to preside in said committee shall be appointed by the president.
47. All amendments made to the original proposition in Committee of the Whole shall be incorporated with it and so reported.
48. The rules of proceeding in the Board of Council shall be observed in Committee of the Whole so far as they are applicable.
49. A motion for the rising of the committee shall always be in order, unless a member is speaking, and shall be decided without debate.

1. In all joint meetings of the two boards the president of the Board of Aldermen shall preside, and the proceedings conducted as nearly as may be according to the rules of the Board of Aldermen.

2. In every case when an amendment of any ordinance or resolution is agreed to in one board and not assented to in the other board, if either board shall request a conference and the other board appoint a committee to confer, said committee shall, at a convenient time to be agreed upon by their chairman, meet and consult together, and report their action to their respective boards.

3. When a message shall be sent from either board, it shall be announced at the door by the messenger, and shall be respectfully communicated to the president by the person by whom it is sent.

4. While ordinances, resolutions, etc., are on their passage between the two boards they shall be under the signature of the clerks of their respective boards.

5. After an ordinance or resolution has passed both boards it shall be indorsed by the clerk of each board, over whose signature shall also be stated the respective dates when the same was acted on by the respective boards.

6. When the ordinance and resolution are so indorsed they shall be delivered to the city attorney, whose duty it shall be to examine the same, and approve or disapprove the same by his indorsement thereon, after which, if found duly passed and in conformity with the law, they shall be presented to the mayor for his approval, when by the charter he is required to approve, and shall also be presented for the signature of the president of each board of the Council.

AN ACT CONCERNING THE DEPOSITS OF THE SINKING FUND OF THE
CITY OF LOUISVILLE.

§ 1. That the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund of the city
of Louisville shall deposit the funds in their hands as commission-
ers in some incorporated bank, State or national, located and doing

missioners aforesaid shall give bond, with good and sufficient se-
curity, to secure the said commissioners the payment of all moneys
and other things of value deposited by them with such bank; and
upon such bond recovery may be had for any breach of the condi-
tions thereof by suit in any court of competent jurisdiction.
§ 2. All laws in conflict with this act are hereby repealed.
§ 3. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

FOR THE PURPOSE OF IMPROVING SAME AT THE COST IN WHOLE OR

and are hereby, created a body-corporate and politic, under the name of the Carrollton and Louisville Railroad Company, with perpetual succession, and by the name and style shall be capable of taking, purchasing, holding, leasing, selling, and conveying real and personal estate, so far as may be necessary or expedient hereinafter mentioned, and in their corporate name may sue and be sued, contract and be contracted with; have a common seal, which they may renew or alter at pleasure, and may have and exercise all rights, powers, privileges, and immunities necessary or proper to be exercised in order to carry into effect the objects and purposes of this act.

The said company shall have power to construct and operate a line of railway of standard guage, with all the necessary and proper sidings, switches, and branches, extending from the city of Louisville, in Jefferson County, to the town of Carrollton, in Carroll County, on such line as may be deemed by said company the most eligible and practicable route between the said terminal points, and with right and power to construct and operate branch lines to such points as the said company may from time to time deem proper and advisable.

§ 3. The incorporators, or a majority of them, may, at any time after one week's previous notice, by publication in a newspaper of general circulation in Carrollton or in the city of Louisville, open books for subscriptions to the capital stock of said company, which subscriptions shall be made on such terms as to payment as may be previously prescribed by the said incorporators or a majority of them. Shares of stock in said company shall be deemed to be personal property, transferable on the books of the company as may be prescribed by the by-laws. At all meetings of stockholders each share of stock shall be entitled to one vote, and stockholders may vote in person or by proxy.

Said company is authorized to render aid and assistance to any company now or hereafter created for the purpose of erecting a bridge over the Ohio River, at or near Carrollton, Kentucky, either by subscribing to the stock or purchasing the bonds of such company, or in any other manner and to such an extent as its directors may deem proper.

§ 13. The directors of said company may enact and enforce such by-laws for the government of said company and its officers, agents, servants, and employes as they deem proper or necessary, and may alter and amend the same at pleasure: *Provided*, That said by-laws shall not be inconsistent with the Constitution or laws of this State or the United States.

§ 14. Said company shall have the right and power to connect with any other line of connecting railway now or hereafter constructed in either of the counties aforesaid, and operate its railway in connection with such other line of connecting railway, or use a part or all of the same on such terms and conditions as may be mutually agreed on.

entries.ocr_text:
CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 103

§ 15. Said company shall have the right and power to construct and operate its road upon and over the streets and highways of the city of Louisville, from its station or stations in said city to the corporate limits thereof; but the said company shall not use or occupy any of the streets, alleys, or highways of the city of Louisville, or enter said city with its railroad except by and with the consent of the Common Council of said city to be first obtained, and only in accordance with such ordinance as the Common Council of said city may enact for that purpose. Said company shall have the power and authority to subscribe for and hold stock in any company created and organized for the purpose of constructing a union passenger station in that city, or may contract with said company for the use of such passenger station, and may, in like manner, contract with any other railway company for the use of its property in said city for terminal facilities.

§ 16. All actions or proceedings instituted by the said company for the condemnation of property for any of the uses herein mentioned shall be regulated and governed by the General Statutes relating to actions or proceedings for condemnation by writ of *ad quod damnum*.

§ 17. Said company may further acquire and hold, by gift, purchase, or condemnation, such land adjacent to its line of railway as may be necessary for the construction of machine and repair shops, and may construct and operate thereon shops and other belongings necessary to be used by it in constructing and repairing its cars and other equipments.

§ 18. That the right and privilege is hereby conferred on said company to build a bridge across Kentucky River where said road crosses the same, which shall be for the use of said road: *Provided, however*, It shall be, when built, as high above low-water mark as the bridge built by the Louisville, Lexington and Cincinnati Railroad near Worthville, in Carroll County, is.

§ 19. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved May 18, 1886.

104 CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE TOWN OF SOUTH LOUISVILLE, IN JEFFERSON COUNTY. § 1. That the town of South Louisville, in the county of Jefferson, shall be, and the same is hereby, incorporated within the following limits, to-wit: Beginning at a point on the westwardly line of the National Turnpike Road, at the intersection with the southern boundary line of the city of Louisville; thence along the north line of the land bought by Schwartz, Wedekemper, etc., from Churchill, to the western line of said land; thence along said line southwardly to the south line of what is known as P Street; thence with said line east to the westward line of the National Turnpike Road; thence along said line to the southwardly line of the twenty-six acre track bought by Jones and Ward from Standiford; thence along said line eastwardly to and across the Louisville & Nashville Railroad right of way to the northeastern line of the fifty-acre tract bought by the Louisville & Nashville Railroad Company from Jones; thence along said line to where it is seven hundred and two feet and three inches from the Louisville Transfer Company's centre line; thence on a prolongation of said northeastern line, in same direction, northwardly, to where it would intersect the northward line of the Lilly land; thence along said line westwardly to what would be the western line of Brook Street if extended; thence south on said line to where it intersects the north line of the Jones and Ward land; thence along said line westwardly to the point of beginning. § 2. Browne, and Peter Lesch are hereby appointed to act as the first board of trustees of said town, who shall continue in office until their successors are elected and qualified, with power to fill any vacancy that may occur in their number. CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 105

successors are elected and qualified; and at said election the qualified voters of said town are entitled to vote, subject to the same rules and regulations now existing or hereafter made by law. The trustees so elected shall, before they enter upon the duties of their offices, take an oath truly and faithfully to perform their duties as trustees. Said trustees and their successors in office shall have the power to enact all by-laws and ordinances necessary to the improvement, regulation, well-being, and advancement of the interests of said town: *Provided*, The same be not inconsistent with the Constitution or laws of the United States or of this State. They may elect a president of the board of trustees. He shall have jurisdiction concurrent with justices of the peace of Jefferson County, and to try all misdemeanors committed in the town or violations of town ordinances or by-laws. Practice in his court and process therefrom shall be controlled by the law applicable to justices of the peace of Jefferson County, and he may charge the same fees and have the same rights and be under the same liabilities of such justices. They may appoint or elect a secretary and appoint or elect an assessor, a collector, and such other officers or agents as they deem necessary to carry out the powers and duties imposed on them by law, and require of them such bonds as they see fit.

§ 4. They are hereby empowered and authorized to receive by gift, donation, or to acquire by purchase or condemnation, any lot or lots of ground in said town for school purposes or making streets or sidewalks, and may contract for the building or purchase of school-houses, making roads, streets, sidewalks, alleys, digging and walling and making wells or pumps, buildings, sewers, and cisterns, laying gas or water pipes, and contracting for a supply of water or gas, and for buying proper fire engines, and keeping in repair all the town's improvements and property.

§ 5. The trustees shall have power to levy and collect an annual tax upon all the land in said town, not to exceed fifty cents upon the one hundred dollars of the cash value of the said land, for the uses and purposes of said town. And said town shall not levy or collect any other tax, but may charge for the purposes herein above stated a license upon any grocery, store, or other place of sale of any articles by retail, not to exceed fifty cents on the one hundred dollars on the gross amount of sales of such grocery, store, or other place of sale by the year; and may fine or prohibit any such place not licensed by them.

106 CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS.

§ 6. The collector shall have the power to collect, by distress or otherwise, the taxes or license levied or fixed on the property or business within said town. Before entering upon his duties said collector shall execute a bond to said trustees, in such penalty as they shall direct, with surety to be approved by them and payable to them, for the faithful collection of said taxes and the payment of same to said trustees or their agent; and for any breach of the conditions of said bond said trustees shall have power and authority to sue said collector and his sureties and enforce payment thereof.

§ 7. Every inhabitant of said town over twenty-one years of age who shall have resided two years in the State or one year in Jefferson County, and sixty days in this town next preceding the election, may be a voter at any town election. The trustees shall appoint judges, sheriffs, and clerks of elections in said town, and be governed therein by the laws in regard to such duties when imposed upon the county court by law in regard to elections in the county.

§ 8. Nothing herein shall authorize said town to regulate the speed of railway trains within its limits, or in any way to interfere with the proper conduct and management of said trains, or with work done or repairs made on the track or road bed of any railroad in said town at any time or upon any day when the work may be necessary in the discretion of the parties charged with said work.

§ 9. *Provided*, That if the boundary of the city of Louisville should be extended so as to include said town of South Louisville, or any part thereof, then, and in that event, the chartered privileges in this act shall become null and void, and the said town of South Louisville and its inhabitants shall be under the same control in every respect of the authority of the city of Louisville as other inhabitants within said city.

§ 10. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved May 17, 1886.

entries.ocr_text:
CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 107

AN ACT INCORPORATING THE LOUISVILLE NATURAL GAS AND HEATING COMPANY.

§ 1. That R. A. Robinson, John T. Moore, Chas. Bremaker, Geo. W. Wicks, J. J. Harbison, Samuel L. Avery, B. F. Camp, J. W. Stine, Wm. Preston, S. A. Foss and W. L. Hardin, W. H. McKnight, J. L. Chilton, H. Verhoff, L. Hart, A. Brandies, W. E. Grinstead, and W. P. Adams, Thos. H. Hays, and their associates, successors, and assigns, be, and they are hereby, incorporated by the name and style of the Louisville Natural Gas and Heating Company, and shall by such name have perpetual succession, with all the rights and privileges of a body-politic and corporate, to contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued; to have and use a common seal, and the same to alter and amend at pleasure; to take, hold, own, and in anywise alien property, real, personal, and mixed, necessary to the conducting of its business; and to establish by laws and make rules and regulations for the management of the affairs of said company, not inconsistent with the Constitution or laws of this State or of the United States.

§ 2. The business of said corporation shall be conducted by a board of directors, of not less than nine or more than thirteen directors, who shall be elected for a term of one year and until their successors are elected and qualified. At a meeting of the stockholders of said corporation, to be held on the first Tuesday in May next after the passage of this act, in the city of Louisville, and annually thereafter on said day. The directors so chosen shall elect a president, vice-president, secretary, and treasurer; the president and vice-president to be chosen from their number. The meetings of said board shall be called by the president, or, in case of his absence or inability to act, by the vice-president, or in case of his absence also or inability to act, by a written call of not less than one-half the directors. The powers, duties, and compensation of the president and other officers of the company shall be prescribed by the board of directors. In all meetings of the stockholders they shall vote in proportion to their stock, in person or by proxy, duly authorized in writing signed by them. Of all meetings of stockholders (whether called by the board of directors, as they may be, or the annual meetings aforesaid), five days' previous notice shall be given to the stockholders, by insertions in a daily newspaper published in the city of Louisville.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall be one million five hundred thousand dollars, divided into shares of one hundred dollars each, which shall be deemed personal property, and subscribed, paid for, and paid in, and transferable in the manner prescribed in the by-laws of said company. The stockholders of said company may, by a two-thirds vote at a meeting called for that purpose, increase said stock not exceeding five hundred thousand dollars.

§ 4. Said company is empowered to develop, produce, utilize, convey, and sell, or in any otherwise dispose of petroleum, natural gas, and salt water, or any of them, which may be upon or come from any of the lands which said company may own, lease, or control, in the counties of Jefferson, Bullitt, and Hardin, in this Commonwealth, or upon which, for any of said purposes, or do any of the acts in this section mentioned, it may have or acquire the right. And to these ends said company is empowered to drill, bore, or in anywise sink and maintain wells for the purpose of producing such petroleum, natural gas, and salt water, or either, and construct, lay, maintain, and operate derricks, buildings, machinery, tanks, pipes, receivers, gasometers, warehouses, manufactories, and any other improvements or appliances in or by means of which such petroleum, gas, and salt water, or either, may be produced, stored, distributed, sold, or utilized, and to construct, lay, and operate conduit pipes of any kind, and any other suitable means for the conveyance, distribution, storing, sale, and utilization of the same, either upon, under, to, or from any of said lands or other places.

§ 5. With the consent of the city of Louisville, by ordinance or resolution of its General Council, and subject to its reasonable regulations aforesaid, said company shall have power to open the ground in any street, alley, park, or other public place within said city's limits, and lay therein, remove, alter, and repair such pipes for so conducting and distributing said natural gas, restoring such ground, however, and any pavement or other improvement thereon, to as good condition as before within a reasonable time thereafter; and in doing the acts in this section mentioned, said company shall cause no injury to any gas or water pipe, or connection, or sewer laid within said ground, and shall be liable in damages for any such injury to said city, or any other corporation or person sustaining the same.

The levy shall be made by separate ordinance for each year. Any person interested in the property on which the tax is a lien may, within thirty days after the publication of the ordinance in any newspaper of said city, file a written complaint in the assessor's office, stating the parcel which he conceives to have been assessed beyond its fair cash value, and how far in excess thereof; and in all cases in which such written complaint is not filed within such thirty days, the present assessment shall become final and binding, and the assessor shall issue new bills thereon, and list them with the receiver of city taxes. If written complaints as above be filed within the thirty days, the mayor shall convoke the board of equalization of the current year to meet at the assessor's office, to whom the complaints shall be submitted, and the persons filing them shall be heard by the board, and the board shall, in the manner indicated in section 7 of article 11 of the act hereby amended, proceed to approve or reduce the assessments; and the assessor shall make out and list new bill in accordance with the action of the board. Such receiver shall, without cost to the tenant, have the proper money order entered in one of the said courts and open an account on his books. This payment into court shall be deemed a suit of interpleader, and shall fully discharge the tenant *pro tanto* without regard to the validity of the garnishment or of the tax bill on which it was issued. The city and landlord may litigate over the fund in a summary way, under rules which said courts shall make for that purpose; but either party may insist upon a hearing in a regular action where the amount involved exceeds fifty dollars, exclusive of interest, commission, and costs.
