[Became a law without the approval of the Governor, May 11, 1886.] AN ACT TO AMEND THE CHARTER OF THE CITY OF LOUISVILLE. § 1. No action for damages of any character whatever to either person or property shall be instituted or maintained against said city unless such action be commenced within six months after the ac- crual of the cause of action, nor shall any action for the recovery of money paid to said city in discharge of taxes claimed by said city to be due and owing to it, be instituted or maintained against said city unless the same be commenced within three years after the accrual of the cause of action. Said city shall in every instance deduct and withhold from the amount of its obligations to any person owing or liable to it for taxes, the amount of such taxes, and surrender to such person the canceled tax-bills therefor, which to the amount thereof and interest thereon, if any, shall be a dis- charge of its obligation to such person. * * * * * * * * * § 4. § 1. AN ORDINANCE PRESCRIBING THE MANNER OF IMPROVING STREETS WITH ASPHALTUM PAVEMENT. APPROVED OCTOBER 21, 1884.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That unless otherwise provided for in the ordinance requiring the work to be done, the grading, curbing, and paving of any street with asphaltum pavement, that the General Council may order, shall be done as hereinafter provided.

### GRADING.

§ 2. The surface of the carriage-way shall be worked to the proper grade and cross-section according to the stakes set by the direction of the city engineer, and the drawings on file in his office.

### ROAD-BED.

§ 3. After being properly graded, all vegetable, or mucky material, quick sand, and loam shall be removed to the extent directed by the city engineer, and be replaced with sand, gravel, broken stone, concrete, or good earth filling, as he may direct.

The road-bed shall be rolled with a steam roller until thoroughly compact, and no paving material shall be put down until the road-bed has been finally inspected by the city engineer.

### CURBING.

§ 4. Separating the sidewalks and carriage-way shall be laid two lines of curbstones, true to the stakes set by direction of the city engineer.

The curbstones shall be of an uniform width of six inches, not less than five feet long and eighteen inches in depth, and the top and bottom of each curbstone shall be parallel, and they shall have

GENERAL ORDINANCES.

full square joints fifteen inches in depth, and a draft one inch wide shall be cut around the top and front faces.
The top and exposed part of the front face shall be neatly dressed with a bush-hammer to an even regular face, and the back shall be similarly dressed three inches below the top.
The front face below the gutter line shall be dressed or scabbled so as to make a smooth perpendicular joint with the paving material. The top face shall have a bevel of three-sixteenths of an inch to correspond to the slope of the sidewalk.
Curbstones shall be of good, hard limestone, equal in quality to the curbstone ledge of the city quarry, and shall be free from flaws, dry seams, and cracks.
Corner curbstones shall be placed at intersections of streets and alleys, which shall be the fourth of a circle of twenty-four (24) inches radius at the intersection of streets, and eighteen (18) inches at the intersection of alleys.
Corner stones shall be of the same depth, same quality, and dressed in a similar manner to the straight curbstones.
When flagstone gutters are used, the curbstones shall be twenty-two (22) inches in depth.

FLAGSTONE GUTTERS.

§ 5. When directed by the General Council in the ordinance requiring the work to be done, the gutter may consist of flagstones not less than five (5) feet long, eight (8) inches deep, and of fifteen (15) and eighteen (18) inches in width, to be laid alternately as stretchers, abutting against the curbstones.
The top face of the flagstones shall be neatly dressed with a bush-hammer to a smooth, regular surface. The end and side faces shall be dressed smooth and square so as to form joints not exceeding one-eighth (1/8) of an inch, opposite faces shall be parallel, and the intersecting joints at right angles. The flagstone shall be laid on a bed of clean, sharp sand six (6) inches in depth, and the joints between consecutive flags and between the flag and curb shall be grouted with hydraulic cement, mixed according to the directions of the city engineer.
The flags shall be made of the best hard limestone, free from seams, cracks, and other defects, and must in every respect equal in workmanship and quality the specimens on exhibition at the office of the city engineer.

GENERAL ORDINANCES. 121

STREET-CAR TRACKS AND GRANITE PAVEMENT.

§ 6. Upon streets with street-car tracks the necessary additional grading to bed the cross-ties will be done as designated by plans. The pavement between the rails will consist of granite blocks laid on a foundation of concrete mixed as described in the following section.

Said foundation of concrete shall in thickness equal the depth of the cross-ties used, and the top surface of the concrete when thoroughly rammed shall be flush with the top surface of said cross-ties. Upon this foundation, including the cross-ties, will be spread a layer of clean, sharp sand of sufficient depth to bed the granite blocks and allow them to be rammed into their true position, as indicated by the plans.

The granite blocks are classified as first class and second class, according to their dimensions in inches, as follows:

- First Class—Length, 6 to 12 inches; width, 3 to 4½ inches; depth, 6 to 7 inches.
- Second Class—Length, 5 to 9 inches; width, 2½ to 4½ inches; depth, 4½ to 6½ inches; and either class may be used as the city engineer may determine.

The granite blocks are to be laid as headers in courses of even width at right angles to the rails, excepting a line on the outside of the outside rails, which shall be laid as headers and stretchers alternately. The blocks laid between the rails shall break joints, with a lap of at least two (2) inches.

After the blocks have been laid, coarse gravel, none of which will pass through a sieve with one-quarter inch meshes, will be swept into the joints until they are thoroughly filled. The blocks will then be thoroughly rammed to place and shall present a smooth, even surface, true to the grade and cross-section as shown upon the drawings.

The joints between the granite blocks will be completely filled with a hot cement, obtained from the direct distillation of coal-tar, and of the consistency ordinarily numbered between five and six.

Said cement will be heated in an iron kettle on the work to 350° Fahrenheit, and will be tested in the buckets from which it is poured into the joints, and none less than 325° Fahrenheit will be allowed to be poured.

GENERAL ORDINANCES.

CONCRETE FOUNDATION.

§ 7. Upon the surface prepared, as specified in sections 2 and 3, a foundation consisting of broken stone, hydraulic cement, and sand, will be laid six inches deep.

The concrete will be made as follows: One and a half measures of hydraulic cement, which shall have a tensile strength of forty pounds to the square inch when mixed pure and allowed to stand twenty-four hours, and a fineness of eighty-five per cent., using standard mesh. Three measures of clean, sharp sand, entirely free from loam and particles of coal, and equal in quality and fineness to specimens on file in the city engineer's office, will be thoroughly mixed dry and then made into mortar, with the least possible quantity of water.

Seven measures of broken stone, free from dust or dirt, drenched with water, will then be mixed thoroughly with mortar until each piece of stone is completely coated. The concrete will then be spread and rammed until free mortar appears on the surface.

The whole operation of mixing and laying each batch of concrete will be performed as expeditiously as possible, with the use of a sufficient number of skilled men. The upper surface will be made exactly parallel with the top surface of the pavement when completed. No gravel will be used in the concrete, but only fragments of stone with rough faces obtained by fracture, and measuring not more than two and one-half inches in the largest dimensions.

After the concrete is laid, it must be protected from the sun and rain until the paving material is put down.

THE WEARING SURFACE.

§ 8. The wearing surface will be composed of—
First—Refined Trinidad or Cuban asphaltum.
Second—Heavy petroleum oil.
Third—Fine sand, containing not more than one per cent. of hydrosilicate of alumina.
Fourth—Fine powder of carbonate of lime.

The asphalt must be refined, and brought to a standard of purity of ninety per cent.

The Cuban asphaltum contains a greater percentage of asphaltene and less petrolene, and, therefore, requires a greater percent-

GENERAL ORDINANCES. 123 age of heavy petroleum oil to make an asphaltic cement of a similar quality as to its malleability. The heavy petroleum oil (which may be the residuum by distillation of petroleum oils) as found in the market generally contains water, light oils, coke, and a gummy substance soluble in water. This petroleum oil must be free from all impurities and brought to a specific gravity of from 18 to 22 degrees Beaume and a fire test of 250 degrees Fahrenheit. By melting and mixing these two hydro-carbons, petroleum oil and asphaltum, the matrix of the pavement called asphaltic cement is manufactured, which cement shall have a fire test of 250 degrees Fahrenheit, and at the temperature of 60 degrees Fahrenheit shall have a specific gravity of 1.19. The cement will be mixed in the following proportions by weight, with Trinidad asphalt: Refined asphaltum, 100 parts; heavy petroleum oil, twenty parts. With Cuban asphalt: Refined asphaltum, 100 parts; heavy petroleum oil, thirty parts. The purity of the asphaltum shall be ascertained and the quality of heavy petroleum oil to be added to it shall be in accordance with the percentage of pure asphaltum it contains when crude, whether Cuban or Trinidad. The asphaltic cement being made as above specified: The pavement mixture will be made of the following materials and proportions by weight:
Material Proportion
Asphaltic cement From 13 to 16
Sand From 72 to 67
Pulverized carbonate of lime From 15 to 17
100 100
In order to make the pavement homogeneous the proportion of asphaltic cement must be varied according to the quality of the sand. The city engineer and his inspectors will make frequent inspections of the work where the paving mixture is manufactured, and will specify the exact amount of asphaltic cement between the limits above given which shall be used in each batch. If the proportions of the mixture are varied in any manner from those specified by the city engineer the mixture will be rejected and condemned, and if already placed on the street it will be removed and replaced at the expense of the contractor. GENERAL ORDINANCES.

The sand and asphaltic cement will be heated separately to about 300° Fahrenheit.
The pulverized carbonate of lime when cold is mixed with the hot sand in the required proportions, and is then mixed with the asphaltic cement at their required temperature and in the proper proportions in an apparatus that will effect the perfect mixture.

CUSHION COAT.

§ 9. The pavement mixture prepared as above described will be placed on the foundation in two coats.
The first coat, called the cushion coat, will contain from two to four per cent. more asphaltic cement than given above.
It will be laid to such depth as will give a thickness of half an inch after being compressed by a steam roller of not less than six tons in weight.

SURFACE COAT.

§ 10. The second coat, called the surface coat, prepared as specified in section 8, will be laid on the cushion coat. It will be brought to the ground in carts at a temperature of about 250 degrees Fahrenheit, and if the temperature of the air is less than 50 degrees Fahrenheit the contractor must provide iron carts with heating apparatus in order to maintain the proper temperature of the mixture. It will be carefully spread by means of hot iron rakes in such a manner as to give a uniform and regular surface, and to such depth that after having received its ultimate compression of two-fifths it will have a thickness of two inches, and will then conform to the stakes set for the grade of the street longitudinally and arch of the street transversely.
As soon as the mixture is spread and raked it will be compressed with a hand roller, after which a small amount of hydraulic cement will be swept over it, and then it will be thoroughly compressed with a steam roller weighing not less than 250 pounds to the inch run, the rolling being continued for not less than five hours for every 1000 yards surface.

PAINTING THE GUTTERS.

§ 11. In order to make the gutters, which are consolidated but little by traffic, entirely impervious to water they shall be painted with a hot coat of asphaltic cement for a width of two feet next the curb and smoothed with hot smoothing irons.

## MATERIALS.

§ 12. All materials will be subject to inspection and must be equal in quality to specimens on file in the city engineer's office.

The degree of fineness of sand and limestone will be determined by testing with screens on file in the city engineer's office.

The powdered carbonate of lime will be of such degree of fineness that 15 per cent. by weight of the entire mixture for the pavement shall be an impalpable powder of limestone, and the whole of it shall pass through a number 26 screen.

The sand will be of such degree of fineness that the whole of it shall pass through a number 20 screen, and that none of it will pass through a number 80 screen.

## GENERAL CONDITIONS.

§ 13. The contractor shall prosecute his work in a prompt and orderly manner, and when required by the city engineer shall discharge incompetent employes and workmen.

The use of the street or any part of it by individuals or the public, shall not entitle the contractor to pay for the work done by him, unless it shall be received by the General Council.

## GUARANTEE.

§ 14. The contractor shall guarantee the faithful performance of his contract according to this ordinance, and the pavement therein specified, and the materials composing the same, shall be kept in good repair for the period of ten (10) years from the completion of the work and its acceptance by the General Council, and to protect the city as to the character of said work and material; and such repairs as may be needed (the city engineer to be the judge), the contractor shall deposit bonds of the city of Louisville or United States bonds amounting to ten per cent. of the original contract price of the entire work, with the city treasurer, who shall hold the same, principal and interest to be applied as far as need be in the necessary repairs to said work, and at the end of said ten (10) years the unexpended balance, if any, to be subject to the order of said contractor.

§ 15. Ordinance No. 635, entitled “An ordinance prescribing the manner of improving streets with asphaltic metal pavement,” approved September 6, 1875, be and is hereby repealed.

GENERAL ORDINANCES.
133

AN ORDINANCE CHANGING THE NAME OF THAT PORTION OF FIFTH STREET BETWEEN KENTUCKY STREET AND ORMSBY AVENUE TO "GARVIN PLACE." APPROVED OCTOBER 28, 1884.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That the name of that portion of Fifth Street situated between Kentucky Street and Ormsby Avenue, in the city of Louisville, be changed, and that said street be called "Garvin Place."
§ 2. This ordinance to take effect on and after its passage.

---

AN ORDINANCE TO LICENSE WHOLESALE DEALERS IN DRY GOODS, CLOTHING, DRUGS, MEDICINES, WARES, OR MERCHANDISE OF ANY KIND. APPROVED NOVEMBER 2, 1884.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That it shall be unlawful for any person, firm, or corporation to do business in the city of Louisville as a wholesale dealer in dry goods, clothing, drugs, medicines, wares, or merchandise of any kind, without first obtaining a license from the Sinking Fund for doing such business.
§ 2. The license tax shall be graded at the rate of one dollar per thousand upon every thousand dollars of yearly sales at wholesale, but no license tax shall exceed twenty-five hundred dollars.
§ 3. That it shall be the duty of merchants and dealers whose sales exceed the amounts authorized by their license to take an additional license to cover said sales, and it shall be the duty of the chief license inspector to issue such license upon the payment of the difference between the amount received for the license theretofore issued and the one then issued, and to cancel the old license. If any merchant or dealer shall continue to sell beyond the amount authorized by his, her, or their license for thirty days, the chief license inspector shall assess and collect from such merchant or dealer the rate of license next above the one he has taken out; and such merchant or dealer shall not be entitled to a license authorizing any kind of business whatever in the city of Louisville until he has paid said additional assessment: *Provided, however*, This section shall not apply to licenses granted before the passage of this ordinance.

GENERAL ORDINANCES.

§ 4. That for the purpose of making a proper classification of applicants for license under this ordinance, the chief license inspector or his assistants shall require of each applicant a statement under oath as to the amount of his, her, or their sales for the preceding year, and no license shall be issued until such sworn statement is made and filed with the chief license inspector.

§ 5. That if any merchant or dealer shall do business as aforesaid without a license, or make sales beyond the amount authorized by his, her, or their license, he, she, or they shall be fined not less than five nor more than fifty dollars for each day that said business may be carried on without license, or beyond the amount authorized by his, her, or their license.

§ 6. An ordinance entitled "An ordinance regulating and establishing the rates of merchants' licenses," approved August 27, 1884, is hereby repealed.

---

AN ORDINANCE CONCERNING THE TAXES OF THE FISCAL YEAR ENDING AUGUST 31, 1885. APPROVED NOVEMBER 22, 1884.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That the following *ad valorem* tax is hereby levied for the fiscal year ending August 31, 1885, on lands, improvements, and such personality as is designated in section 5 of article 1 of an act to revise and amend the tax laws of the city of Louisville, approved May 12, 1884, in each case upon each one hundred dollars in value:
*   For city purposes, proper, eighty-five cents.
*   For the support of the high schools and common schools, thirty cents.
*   To meet principal and interest of bonds issued to the E. and P. R. R. Co., and to the L., N. A. and St. Louis R. R. Co., thirty cents.
*   For the use of the Sinking Fund, forty cents.
*   For the House of Refuge, five cents.
*   For cleaning and repairing sewers, two cents.
*   For cleaning and repairing public ways (and including footways) and crossings, thirty cents.
*   For reconstruction of carriage-ways of streets and alleys, fourteen cents.

The bills for the year shall be made out by the assessor upon blanks having on them, in print, both in detail and in totals, the rates of taxes *ad valorem* and the head tax as above; and in casting up the amounts chargeable in any one bill only the following amounts shall be stated separately: the sum chargeable as a tax on lands, improvements, and designated personalty; the sum chargeable on investments or on acre property, and the head tax.

§ 5. The receiver shall keep his sheets and books for the year so as to show separately the moneys received under the three heads named in the preceding section; and when he reports his collection he shall, by calculation, ascertain the sums belonging to each fund and purpose, as named in the first, second, and third sections hereof. He shall also keep on his sheets and books a column for discount, for interest, and for costs and commissions.

§ 6. The head tax bills against persons who are not assessed for any property, real or personal, shall be made out by the assessor, and for doing so the city shall be put to no expense beyond the printing and binding of the bills, and he shall deliver them to the tax receiver as soon as they can be completed.

§ 7. The assessor is directed to write across the face of each tax bill, other than the head tax bills mentioned in section six, the following words: "Original tax bill for the fiscal year ending August 31, 1885. ———, Assessor," in red ink. He may cause a fac simile of these words, including his signature, to be engraved upon a die, and have these words stamped across the face of each bill under his supervision.

AN ORDINANCE TO LICENSE POOL TABLES. APPROVED NOVEMBER 22, 1884.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That each and every pool table in this city, kept for hire or profit, or compensation of any kind, shall pay a license of fifty dollars per annum into the Sinking Fund: *Provided, however*, If the establishments in which such table or tables are kept do not sell or allow to be sold or drank on the premises any spirituous, fermented, or vinous liquors, then the rate of such table or tables shall be twenty-five dollars each.

§ 2. Any person who shall keep a pool table without having first obtained a license, as provided in section 1 of this ordinance, shall be fined for each offense not less than twenty-five nor more than fifty dollars.

§ 3. That ordinance No. ———, entitled "An ordinance to license pool tables," approved August 27, 1884, be and the same is hereby repealed.

---

GENERAL ORDINANCES. 137

cines, wares, or merchandise of any kind, without first obtaining a license from the Sinking Fund to carry on said business: *Provided*, That no person, firm, or corporation shall be licensed under this ordinance to sell spirituous, vinous, or fermented liquors to be drank on the premises of the place of sale: and *provided, further*, That the provisions of this ordinance shall not apply to such business, trades, or occupations as are otherwise specially provided to be licensed under the provisions of section nine, article one, of an act entitled "An act to revise and amend the tax laws of the city of Louisville," approved May 12, 1884.

§ 2. Subject to the provisions above, contained in section one, every person, firm, or corporation, doing business in this city as retail grocers, or retail dealers in dry goods, clothing, drugs, medicines, wares, or merchandise of any kind, shall be divided into twelve classes, and the rate of license which shall be paid into the treasury of the Sinking Fund for each class shall be graded as follows, to-wit:

*   For the first class, $100 per annum.
*   For the second class, $90 per annum.
*   For the third class, $80 per annum.
*   For the fourth class, $70 per annum.
*   For the fifth class, $60 per annum.
*   For the sixth class, $50 per annum.
*   For the seventh class, $40 per annum.
*   For the eighth class, $30 dollars per annum.
*   For the ninth class, $20 dollars per annum.
*   For the tenth class, $15 per annum.
*   For the eleventh class, $10 per annum.
*   For the twelfth class, $5 per annum.

§ 3. Any retail grocer or retail dealer in dry goods, clothing, drugs, medicines, wares, or merchandise of any kind, subject to the provisions and exceptions contained in section one of this ordinance, having a first class license, shall be authorized to sell by retail, during the continuance thereof, any amount.

The holder of a second-class license shall be authorized to sell by retail not exceeding $45,000 per annum.

The holder of a third-class license shall be authorized to sell by retail not exceeding $40,000 per annum.

The holder of a fourth-class license shall be authorized to sell by retail not exceeding $35,000 per annum.

§ 4. That it shall be the duty of merchants and dealers whose sales exceed the amounts authorized by their license to take an additional license to cover said sales, and it shall be the duty of the chief license inspector to issue such license upon the payment of the difference between the amount received for the license theretofore issued and the one then issued, and to cancel the old license. If any merchant or dealer shall continue to sell beyond the amount authorized by his, her, or their license for thirty days the chief license inspector shall assess and collect from such merchant or dealer the rate of license next above the one he has taken out; and said merchant or dealer shall not be entitled to a license authorizing any kind of business whatever in the city of Louisville until he has paid said additional assessment: *Provided, however*, This section shall not apply to licenses granted before the passage of this ordinance.

§ 5. That for the purpose of making a proper classification of applicants for license under this ordinance, the chief license inspector, or his assistants, shall require of each applicant a statement under oath as to the amount of his, her, or their sales for the preceding year, and no license shall be issued until such sworn statement is made and filed with the chief license inspector..

AN ORDINANCE CONCERNING DEPUTY RECEIVERS OF CITY TAXES. APPROVED NOVEMBER 28, 1884.

§ 1. *Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville*: That two deputy receivers of city taxes shall hereafter be nominated by the receiver of city taxes, and elected and confirmed by the General Council in joint session, the term of each to cease with that of their principal, or whenever the latter by writing under his hand revokes the nomination of any such deputy; such writing is to be filed with the mayor.

§ 2. Such deputy may do any of the acts which the tax law of May 12, 1884, authorizes such deputy to perform, only, however, under the assignment to him by the receiver, and shall otherwise assist him in the performance of his duties, as directed by him. They shall deliver every day to the receiver all moneys received or collected by them on that day.

§ 3. Before entering on the performance of his duties each deputy shall give bond with approved surety in the sum of five thousand dollars for his faithful conduct; and the receiver of city taxes shall also be responsible upon his own official bond for any money which such deputy may receive or collect on tax bills.

§ 4. The deputies provided for in this ordinance shall be compensated in the sum of seventy-five dollars per month; and the compensation heretofore provided by resolution for clerk hire shall go toward the payment of such deputies, so that no additional charge shall arise to the city from the institution of deputy receivers.

§ 5. The joint finance committee may at any time by resolution revoke the appointment of any deputy for any cause deemed sufficient. Vacancies arising thus or otherwise shall be filled as provided for in section 1.

AN ORDINANCE AUTHORIZING THE JEFFERSONVILLE, MADISON, AND INDIANAPOLIS RAILROAD COMPANY TO CONSTRUCT RAILROAD TRACKS ON THE PUBLIC STREETS AND ALLEYS NORTH OF MAIN STREET, AND BETWEEN THIRTEENTH STREET AND FIFTEENTH STREET. APPROVED JANUARY 3, 1885.

*Be it ordained by the General Council of the city of Louisville:*

First—That the Jeffersonville, Madison, and Indianapolis Railroad Company, in order to utilize the ground heretofore and recently acquired by it for railroad purposes, and in order to afford better facilities for it and other railroad companies having their yards located in the vicinity of Fourteenth and Main streets, for the transaction of their business, be and it is hereby authorized to take up and relay the tracks now in the public streets and alleys north of Main Street and between Thirteenth and Fifteenth streets, and to construct and operate new and additional tracks over and across said public streets and alleys, so that the system of tracks when completed shall conform to the map hereto attached, and a copy of which is required to be filed and safely kept in the office of the city engineer, and properly identified as the map referred to in this ordinance.

Second—The work of constructing said tracks shall be done subject to the supervision and approval of the city engineer or his assistant, and wherever the surface of a public highway is broken or disturbed, said company shall repair the same and replace it in as good condition as it was before, and wherever the said tracks cross a highway used by vehicles, the space between the rails and for one foot on either side of the rails shall be, by the respective companies owning said tracks, kept covered with plank so that the surface thereof shall be on a level with the top of the rails.

Third—The said tracks when so constructed shall be operated in all respects subject to the terms and conditions of the ordinances and contracts under which the present tracts were constructed and have been operated.

Fourth—The rights herein granted are subject to the following provisos: That the westerly track on Fourteenth Street, as shown on the map mentioned in section first above, between Portland Avenue and Rowan Street, shall be constructed so as to enter the

freight yard or connected with tracks of the Ohio and Mississippi Railway Company, and so as to be used for transporting freight and passengers to and from said freight yards and the passenger depot of said Ohio and Mississippi Railway Company. Said track between Portland Avenue and Rowan Street shall be constructed and owned jointly by the Kentucky and Indiana Bridge Company and the Louisville Bridge Company, and each of said bridge companies shall have free and unimpeded use of said track for the purposes aforesaid by all railroad trains using said bridges respectively.

If said track should prove to be insufficient for the joint use of said bridge companies and of the railroad trains using their said bridges, then the Jeffersonville, Madison, and Indianapolis Railroad Company and the Louisville Bridge Company shall give to the Kentucky and Indiana Bridge Company the exclusive use of said westerly track above mentioned between Portland Avenue and Rowan Street for the purposes aforesaid, or shall give to it the right to connect with and use other tracks of the said parties for the purpose of transacting business with the freight yard and passenger depot of the Ohio and Mississippi Railway Company. Said track shall not be used except for the purpose of conducting business with the Ohio and Mississippi Railway Company, and shall not be used by either party for standing cars.

If the other parties named herein should refuse to accept the provisions of this ordinance, the Kentucky and Indiana Bridge Company may accept and act under the same for the purpose of constructing and operating said track along Fourteenth Street to the freight yard and passenger depot of said Ohio and Mississippi Railway Company.

Provided, That no track on Fourteenth Street, between Portland Avenue and Rowan Street, shall be shifted westerly of where it is now located, nor shall any track be laid in Fourteenth Street, between Portland Avenue and Rowan Street, west of the most westerly track now located there, and that the short piece of track now laid in said street west of the middle track, the building of which is enjoined, be removed from said street, and that the map filed with the ordinance be modified accordingly.
